premise_sentence: In the field of quantum technologies, two large consortium projects were supported under the Excellent Research call of the OP JAK program – QEENTEC led by the Institute of Scientific Instruments of the Czech Academy of Sciences and bringing together the Institute of Photonics and Electronics, the Jaroslav Heyrovský Institute of Physical Chemistry, Palacký University in Olomouc, and CESNET; and the QM4ST consortium project led by the University of West Bohemia in Plzeň, bringing together Charles University, Masaryk University, and the Brno University of Technology.
premise_paragraph:
In 2016, t h e Czech Republic launched the National Initiative for Quantum Technologies, which was launched in connection with the preparation of the European EU Quantum Flagship initiative and aims to promote and support the development of quantum technologies. The topic of quantum technologies is also included in the Czech Optical Cluster, founded in 2017, where optical quantum technologies represent one of the pillars. The cluster, which already has 43 members, connects the spheres of research, education, and industry and is also a way to establish international cooperation with industry clusters abroad. Thanks to the link between the further development of semiconductor technologies and quantum technologies, the topic of quantum technologies is also emphasized on the platform of the Czech National Semiconductor Cluster.
Research teams from the Czech Republic are actively involved in selected European research programs and initiatives in the field of quantum technologies. For example, as part of EuroHPC JU, a VQL quantum computer will be installed in 2025 at the IT4Innovations VŠB-TUO national supercomputing center. It is funded by the EU and the international LUMI-Q consortium, which brings together partners from nine EU countries. The quantum computer will have at least 24 qubits based on superconducting technology and connected in a "one-to-all" topology. For the implementation of hybrid quantum-classical tasks, it will be integrated with the EuroHPC Karolina supercomputer operated at the IT4Innovations center. In 2025, VŠB-TUO became a member of the European association QuIC (Quantum Industry Consortium), which seeks to accelerate the development of quantum technologies and strengthen Europe's role as a global leader in this dynamically developing field.
Through the EuroQCI initiative, the Czech Republic has joined research leading to the construction of a secure quantum communication backbone network in the EU. This project is considered the most extensive project in the field of quantum communication to date and represents the culmination of decades of research, development, and testing. In the Czech Republic, it is coordinated by CyberSecurity Hub, z.ú. and a consortium of Czech universities is working on it.
On January 29, 2024, the Czech Technical University in Prague (CTU) connected to IBM's quantum computer (400 qubits) and signed a cooperation agreement in the field of quantum
technology between other universities and research organizations. CTU also cooperates with seventeen other partners from EU universities and companies on the European Photonic Quantum Computer (Epique) project funded by the European Commission. The Department of Coherent Optics of the Institute of Scientific Instruments (ÚPT) of the Czech Academy of Sciences is also involved in a number of consortium projects supporting both its own development of quantum ion clocks within the European metrology community and, above all, the implementation and expansion of a network of coherent transmission lines for the distribution of precise time derived from these clocks. ÚPT is very actively involved in the research and development of its own technology and is at the forefront of European efforts in this field.
Another important project is the Horizon Europe program project Center for Artificial Intelligence and Quantum Computing in Systems Brain Research (CLARA), co-financed by the Jan Amos Komenský Operational Program (OP JAK), which aims to build an interdisciplinary center of excellence focused on the use of artificial intelligence and quantum computing for research into neurodegenerative diseases. As part of this center, a testbed will be created, which will include quantum computers at VŠB-TUO and the LRZ center in Bavaria and, together with the new HPCQC-AI computing cluster, will enable the implementation of hybrid quantum-classical computations.
In the field of quantum technologies, two large consortium projects were supported under the Excellent Research call of the OP JAK program – QEENTEC led by the Institute of Scientific Instruments of the Czech Academy of Sciences and bringing together the Institute of Photonics and Electronics, the Jaroslav Heyrovský Institute of Physical Chemistry, Palacký University in Olomouc, and CESNET; and the QM4ST consortium project led by the University of West Bohemia in Plzeň, bringing together Charles University, Masaryk University, and the Brno University of Technology. The first project aims to develop hybrid quantum gates for quantum computers. The second project, QM4ST, focuses on research and development, particularly in the modeling and design of new materials, but also on the control of processes that are part of new devices. This creates an opportunity for Czechia to join the world's leading players in the field of quantum chip and computer research and development and thus secure a position in their commercialization, if not in the form of their final production, then at least in the role of suppliers of certain key components.
The Czech Republic is one of the European countries actively involved in the European quantum ecosystem. Cutting-edge research activities are carried out in the fields of quantum computing, quantum communication, quantum sensors, metrology, and quantum materials. Research teams are mainly concentrated at universities and institutes of the Czech Academy of Sciences across the Czech Republic (Figure 2). Important research centers include Palacký University in Olomouc, particularly the Department of Optics at the Faculty of Science, the joint optics laboratory of Palacký University and the Institute of Physics of the Czech Academy of Sciences, and the Czech Institute of Research and Advanced Technologies. Other institutions include the Czech Technical University, particularly the Department of Physics and the Department of Physical Electronics of the Faculty of Nuclear and Physical Engineering, the Faculty of Electrical Engineering, and the Faculty of Biomedical Engineering. At Charles University, it is primarily the Faculty of Mathematics and Physics. At the Czech Academy of Sciences, it is primarily the Institute of Instrumentation, the Institute of Physics, the Institute of Organic Chemistry and Biochemistry, the J. Heyrovský Institute of Physical Chemistry, the Institute of Mathematics, and the Institute of Computer Science. At Masaryk University, it is primarily the Faculty of Science and the Faculty of Informatics. At Mendel University in Brno, it is primarily the Institute of Chemistry and Biochemistry. At the University of West Bohemia in Pilsen, it is primarily the University Institute of New Technologies – Research Center and the Department of Physics of the Faculty of Applied Sciences. At the Brno University of Technology, it is mainly the Faculty of Electrical Engineering and Communication Technologies (Quantum Security Laboratory) and the Faculty of Information Technology. At VŠB-TUO, it is mainly the IT4Innovations National Supercomputing Center and the Faculty of Electrical Engineering and Computer Science. In 2020, Masaryk University, CTU, and Brno University of Technology established a registered institute for cybersecurity, CyberSecurity Hub, z.ú. Other important institutions include CEITEC, ELI ERIC, and the CESNET interest group, whose members include public and state universities and the Czech Academy of Sciences. ČD Telematika also cooperates in the development and implementation of secure quantum communication and infrastructure in particular.
Figure 2: Map of Czech research activities in the field of quantum technologies
In the field of education, there are several educational programs currently being prepared for dozens of students at all levels.
The Czech Technical University in Prague offers a complete university education (Bachelor's, Master's, Ph.D.) in quantum technologies, including participation in the DigiQ project focused on the development of educational programs in the field of quantum technologies within the European education area. In addition, a master's program in quantum informatics was launched here in 2025.
Palacký University in Olomouc offers a wide range of fields and specializations covering many areas of modern quantum optics, the interaction of radiation with atoms, ions, mechanical oscillators, solid-state or superconducting circuits, and their applications in quantum technologies. Charles University teaches a wide range of subjects in the field of quantum sciences in condensed and solid materials, nonlinear optics, low-temperature physics, and a number of related fields. Masaryk University teaches a course in quantum information processing. At VŠB-TUO, subjects such as Introduction to Quantum Computing, Quantum Error Correction, Quantum Computer Programming, and Quantum Algorithms are taught. In addition, a master's program focused on high-performance computing, quantum computing, and artificial intelligence is in preparation, with a planned start date in 2026. The University of Chemistry and Technology in Prague teaches courses in Quantum Chemistry and Quantum Mechanics for Materials Engineering. The University of West Bohemia in Pilsen is preparing a doctoral program in Physics for Quantum Materials.
If we compare the Czech Republic with other countries in all contexts, such as the US, Australia, the UK, the Netherlands, Ireland, Denmark, or Spain, we see an opportunity to support the development of quantum technologies, especially in terms of targeting effective application. Compared to developed countries, the Czech Republic still lags behind in its ability to provide targeted support for quantum science, transfer know-how and technologies from the world of quantum science and research to the world of practice, and in effective and systematic long-term cooperation between universities, institutes of the Czech Academy of Sciences, and the private sector. This situation can be improved by increasing the number of experts capable of working with quantum technologies in companies. This underscores the fundamental importance of education and retraining in this field.
There are few new start-ups or university or academic spin-offs in the Czech Republic that focus on the application of quantum technologies. There are investment funds that focus, among other things, on investments in quantum technologies, such as Tensor Ventures and Presto Ventures. The first companies are also emerging that are attempting to use and create quantum algorithms for their own use. A great opportunity is that there are a number of multinational companies operating in the Czech Republic that are intensively engaged in the development of quantum technologies, primarily in the areas of quantum computers, quantum communication, and quantum sensors and materials. The presence of multinational companies with local branches or research centers that support the development of quantum technologies, for example in the areas of quantum computers, communications, sensors, and materials, creates opportunities for cooperation with Czech entities. Companies such as Microsoft, AWS, Google, and IBM are developing global initiatives that may also be an opportunity for Czech industry and academia. Opportunities for cooperation are also provided by a number of European companies operating in the field of quantum technologies, such as IQM, Pasqal, Quandela, Qilimanjaro, Terra Quantum, ParityQC, and others.
Thanks to its integration into the European Research Area and the presence of renowned research institutions and experts, Czechia has a strong starting position for involvement in the European and global quantum ecosystem. The Czech company Amires s.r.o. administers the important European projects Qu-test and Qu-pilot, which focus on making research capacities available to companies involved in quantum technologies. The opportunity also creates the possibility for Czech entities to participate in "quantum chips pilot lines." This integration offers research institutions and domestic companies the chance to be at the forefront of new technologies and thus become part of the emerging market for quantum applications. It is therefore necessary to find financial resources to cover participation in European and non-European research programs where national co-financing is required, thereby placing the country among the advanced nations. Limited support from public and private funding, a tendency toward secrecy on the part of large companies and their unwillingness to cooperate with domestic research, an insufficient number of educational programs in the field of quantum technologies, and a low number of start-ups and spin-offs are among the main challenges for further development in this area.
Building a quantum sector in the Czech Republic is not only a matter of scientific progress, but also a fundamental opportunity to create a completely new industrial sector with high added value. A key factor for success will be the ability to effectively transfer technology from research institutions to industry and services, where quantum technologies can bring breakthrough solutions and new business opportunities. This transfer is necessary for Czechia to become not only a center of research excellence, but also a major player in the European and global market for quantum applications.
Quantum technologies are globally recognized as a breakthrough development with the potential to radically transform many strategically important areas. Developed countries around the world are therefore investing significant public funds in research and development in this area, with the key objectives being to gain a competitive advantage, ensure national security, and create a strong quantum ecosystem. These investments support not only basic and applied research, education, and talent development, but also the emergence of new technology start-ups and spin-offs and the commercialization of quantum innovations. Therefore, it is necessary to support a culture of founding deep-tech start-ups and spin-offs. Information on the measures taken, strategies, public and private resources, individual quantum opportunities and challenges that advanced countries such as the US52 , the Netherlands53 , Denmark54 , Sweden55, Australia56, the United Kingdom57 , Canada58 , Japan59 , andSouth Korea60 , can be found in the relevant national strategies of these countries.
In the Czech Republic, the potential for applying quantum technologies has been identified primarily in areas that are among the key innovation sectors of the national economy. These include, in particular, technologically advanced industries that use cutting-edge manufacturing technologies, automation, and digitization, where quantum simulations and calculations can significantly streamline the development of materials and manufacturing processes. They can also be used in the semiconductor industry and in the development of advanced chips. They also have applications in the fields of microelectronics, nanophotonics, and thin-film technologies. In cybersecurity
, quantum key distribution is a key tool for protecting sensitive data. High application potential is also expected in the healthcare and pharmaceutical research sectors, particularly in the field of biomolecule modeling and accelerating drug development, as well as in the energy sector, for example in network optimization or research into new types of batteries. Significant opportunities for the use of quantum technologies are also opening up in transportation, logistics, finance, and the defense industry.
However, quantum technologies represent a revolutionary and constantly evolving field whose full application potential is only just beginning to be discovered. Many of the future applications—as was the case with semiconductors and the internet in the past—cannot be accurately predicted today, and overly narrow sector-specific specifications at this stage could limit flexibility and the ability to respond quickly to new opportunities. That is why the Quantum Europe Strategy (2025) does not define specific application sectors in detail, but works with an open framework that allows Member States to dynamically develop quantum technologies according to current technological developments, research capacities, and industrial demand. The national strategy adopts this logic and at the same time reflects the Czech context by identifying general areas with high application potential (e.g., cybersecurity, sensors, materials research, post-quantum cryptography). Specifying priority sectors in too much detail at this stage could lead to premature narrowing of options and loss of synergies across the application sphere. More specific priorities will be set out in a follow-up implementation plan, which will be based on current technological trends, the results of an analysis of national capacities, and feedback from the application sector, and which will be regularly updated. This will ensure the necessary coordination of resources and openness to new innovation opportunities.

premise_sentence:
These immediate actions address the challenges and opportunities identified in this strategy by:
- • improving access to advanced infrastructure, materials and tools.
premise_paragraph:
These immediate actions address the challenges and opportunities identified in this strategy by:
- • improving access to advanced infrastructure, materials and tools. This will drive growth and ensure Australia’s quantum ecosystem’s needs are met now and in the future
- • providing more shared quantum infrastructure for research, development and domestic manufacturing
- • growing a strong local quantum industry with sovereign capability and expertise to drive economic growth, productivity and jobs
- • capitalising on our world‑class infrastructure to make Australia an attractive destination for international talent.
Case study: Access to infrastructure enables quantum start‑ups
(<https://www.aqcircuits.com/>)Analog Quantum Circuits (AQC) is an Australian quantum start‑up focused on superconducting quantum hardware. AQC is based on research in superconducting microwave devices, which was supported by the Australian Research Council through Future Fellowships and the (<https://equs.org/>)ARC Centre of Excellence in Engineered (<https://equs.org/>)Quantum Systems (EQUS).
AQC’s establishment faced 2 major challenges commonly experienced by other start‑ups seeking to commercialise university research. AQC experienced difficulties in securing access to university‑held intellectual property that underpins their research and development. They also experienced challenges accessing the necessary facilities for fabrication, process control and cryogenic measurement to undertake development of advanced hardware.
Gaps in the research and development pipeline create challenges for AQC and other start‑ups seeking to become operational. For example, there are currently no openly accessible industry cryogenic measurement facilities in Australia available for use by quantum technology companies. Where advanced facilities do exist, access can be costly and have long lead times. While universities host some equipment that is industry accessible, this does not cover the full range of requirements for a vibrant quantum technology industry.
A cryogenic dilution refrigerator in the Superconducting Quantum Devices laboratory, at the University of Queensland. Credit: Arkady Feodrov, University of Queensland

Another important project is the Horizon Europe program project Center for Artificial Intelligence and Quantum Computing in Systems Brain Research (CLARA), co-financed by the Jan Amos Komenský Operational Program (OP JAK), which aims to build an interdisciplinary center of excellence focused on the use of artificial intelligence and quantum computing for research into neurodegenerative diseases. As part of this center, a testbed will be created, which will include quantum computers at VŠB-TUO and the LRZ center in Bavaria and, together with the new HPCQC-AI computing cluster, will enable the implementation of hybrid quantum-classical computations.
In the field of quantum technologies, two large consortium projects were supported under the Excellent Research call of the OP JAK program – QEENTEC led by the Institute of Scientific Instruments of the Czech Academy of Sciences and bringing together the Institute of Photonics and Electronics, the Jaroslav Heyrovský Institute of Physical Chemistry, Palacký University in Olomouc, and CESNET; and the QM4ST consortium project led by the University of West Bohemia in Plzeň, bringing together Charles University, Masaryk University, and the Brno University of Technology. The first project aims to develop hybrid quantum gates for quantum computers. The second project, QM4ST, focuses on research and development, particularly in the modeling and design of new materials, but also on the control of processes that are part of new devices. This creates an opportunity for Czechia to join the world's leading players in the field of quantum chip and computer research and development and thus secure a position in their commercialization, if not in the form of their final production, then at least in the role of suppliers of certain key components.
The Czech Republic is one of the European countries actively involved in the European quantum ecosystem. Cutting-edge research activities are carried out in the fields of quantum computing, quantum communication, quantum sensors, metrology, and quantum materials. Research teams are mainly concentrated at universities and institutes of the Czech Academy of Sciences across the Czech Republic (Figure 2). Important research centers include Palacký University in Olomouc, particularly the Department of Optics at the Faculty of Science, the joint optics laboratory of Palacký University and the Institute of Physics of the Czech Academy of Sciences, and the Czech Institute of Research and Advanced Technologies. Other institutions include the Czech Technical University, particularly the Department of Physics and the Department of Physical Electronics of the Faculty of Nuclear and Physical Engineering, the Faculty of Electrical Engineering, and the Faculty of Biomedical Engineering. At Charles University, it is primarily the Faculty of Mathematics and Physics. At the Czech Academy of Sciences, it is primarily the Institute of Instrumentation, the Institute of Physics, the Institute of Organic Chemistry and Biochemistry, the J. Heyrovský Institute of Physical Chemistry, the Institute of Mathematics, and the Institute of Computer Science. At Masaryk University, it is primarily the Faculty of Science and the Faculty of Informatics. At Mendel University in Brno, it is primarily the Institute of Chemistry and Biochemistry. At the University of West Bohemia in Pilsen, it is primarily the University Institute of New Technologies – Research Center and the Department of Physics of the Faculty of Applied Sciences. At the Brno University of Technology, it is mainly the Faculty of Electrical Engineering and Communication Technologies (Quantum Security Laboratory) and the Faculty of Information Technology. At VŠB-TUO, it is mainly the IT4Innovations National Supercomputing Center and the Faculty of Electrical Engineering and Computer Science. In 2020, Masaryk University, CTU, and Brno University of Technology established a registered institute for cybersecurity, CyberSecurity Hub, z.ú. Other important institutions include CEITEC, ELI ERIC, and the CESNET interest group, whose members include public and state universities and the Czech Academy of Sciences. ČD Telematika also cooperates in the development and implementation of secure quantum communication and infrastructure in particular.
Figure 2: Map of Czech research activities in the field of quantum technologies
In the field of education, there are several educational programs currently being prepared for dozens of students at all levels.
The Czech Technical University in Prague offers a complete university education (Bachelor's, Master's, Ph.D.) in quantum technologies, including participation in the DigiQ project focused on the development of educational programs in the field of quantum technologies within the European education area. In addition, a master's program in quantum informatics was launched here in 2025.
Palacký University in Olomouc offers a wide range of fields and specializations covering many areas of modern quantum optics, the interaction of radiation with atoms, ions, mechanical oscillators, solid-state or superconducting circuits, and their applications in quantum technologies. Charles University teaches a wide range of subjects in the field of quantum sciences in condensed and solid materials, nonlinear optics, low-temperature physics, and a number of related fields. Masaryk University teaches a course in quantum information processing. At VŠB-TUO, subjects such as Introduction to Quantum Computing, Quantum Error Correction, Quantum Computer Programming, and Quantum Algorithms are taught. In addition, a master's program focused on high-performance computing, quantum computing, and artificial intelligence is in preparation, with a planned start date in 2026. The University of Chemistry and Technology in Prague teaches courses in Quantum Chemistry and Quantum Mechanics for Materials Engineering. The University of West Bohemia in Pilsen is preparing a doctoral program in Physics for Quantum Materials.
If we compare the Czech Republic with other countries in all contexts, such as the US, Australia, the UK, the Netherlands, Ireland, Denmark, or Spain, we see an opportunity to support the development of quantum technologies, especially in terms of targeting effective application. Compared to developed countries, the Czech Republic still lags behind in its ability to provide targeted support for quantum science, transfer know-how and technologies from the world of quantum science and research to the world of practice, and in effective and systematic long-term cooperation between universities, institutes of the Czech Academy of Sciences, and the private sector. This situation can be improved by increasing the number of experts capable of working with quantum technologies in companies. This underscores the fundamental importance of education and retraining in this field.
There are few new start-ups or university or academic spin-offs in the Czech Republic that focus on the application of quantum technologies. There are investment funds that focus, among other things, on investments in quantum technologies, such as Tensor Ventures and Presto Ventures. The first companies are also emerging that are attempting to use and create quantum algorithms for their own use. A great opportunity is that there are a number of multinational companies operating in the Czech Republic that are intensively engaged in the development of quantum technologies, primarily in the areas of quantum computers, quantum communication, and quantum sensors and materials. The presence of multinational companies with local branches or research centers that support the development of quantum technologies, for example in the areas of quantum computers, communications, sensors, and materials, creates opportunities for cooperation with Czech entities. Companies such as Microsoft, AWS, Google, and IBM are developing global initiatives that may also be an opportunity for Czech industry and academia. Opportunities for cooperation are also provided by a number of European companies operating in the field of quantum technologies, such as IQM, Pasqal, Quandela, Qilimanjaro, Terra Quantum, ParityQC, and others.
Thanks to its integration into the European Research Area and the presence of renowned research institutions and experts, Czechia has a strong starting position for involvement in the European and global quantum ecosystem. The Czech company Amires s.r.o. administers the important European projects Qu-test and Qu-pilot, which focus on making research capacities available to companies involved in quantum technologies. The opportunity also creates the possibility for Czech entities to participate in "quantum chips pilot lines." This integration offers research institutions and domestic companies the chance to be at the forefront of new technologies and thus become part of the emerging market for quantum applications. It is therefore necessary to find financial resources to cover participation in European and non-European research programs where national co-financing is required, thereby placing the country among the advanced nations. Limited support from public and private funding, a tendency toward secrecy on the part of large companies and their unwillingness to cooperate with domestic research, an insufficient number of educational programs in the field of quantum technologies, and a low number of start-ups and spin-offs are among the main challenges for further development in this area.
Building a quantum sector in the Czech Republic is not only a matter of scientific progress, but also a fundamental opportunity to create a completely new industrial sector with high added value. A key factor for success will be the ability to effectively transfer technology from research institutions to industry and services, where quantum technologies can bring breakthrough solutions and new business opportunities. This transfer is necessary for Czechia to become not only a center of research excellence, but also a major player in the European and global market for quantum applications.
Quantum technologies are globally recognized as a breakthrough development with the potential to radically transform many strategically important areas. Developed countries around the world are therefore investing significant public funds in research and development in this area, with the key objectives being to gain a competitive advantage, ensure national security, and create a strong quantum ecosystem. These investments support not only basic and applied research, education, and talent development, but also the emergence of new technology start-ups and spin-offs and the commercialization of quantum innovations. Therefore, it is necessary to support a culture of founding deep-tech start-ups and spin-offs. Information on the measures taken, strategies, public and private resources, individual quantum opportunities and challenges that advanced countries such as the US52 , the Netherlands53 , Denmark54 , Sweden55, Australia56, the United Kingdom57 , Canada58 , Japan59 , andSouth Korea60 , can be found in the relevant national strategies of these countries.
In the Czech Republic, the potential for applying quantum technologies has been identified primarily in areas that are among the key innovation sectors of the national economy. These include, in particular, technologically advanced industries that use cutting-edge manufacturing technologies, automation, and digitization, where quantum simulations and calculations can significantly streamline the development of materials and manufacturing processes. They can also be used in the semiconductor industry and in the development of advanced chips. They also have applications in the fields of microelectronics, nanophotonics, and thin-film technologies. In cybersecurity
, quantum key distribution is a key tool for protecting sensitive data. High application potential is also expected in the healthcare and pharmaceutical research sectors, particularly in the field of biomolecule modeling and accelerating drug development, as well as in the energy sector, for example in network optimization or research into new types of batteries. Significant opportunities for the use of quantum technologies are also opening up in transportation, logistics, finance, and the defense industry.
However, quantum technologies represent a revolutionary and constantly evolving field whose full application potential is only just beginning to be discovered. Many of the future applications—as was the case with semiconductors and the internet in the past—cannot be accurately predicted today, and overly narrow sector-specific specifications at this stage could limit flexibility and the ability to respond quickly to new opportunities. That is why the Quantum Europe Strategy (2025) does not define specific application sectors in detail, but works with an open framework that allows Member States to dynamically develop quantum technologies according to current technological developments, research capacities, and industrial demand. The national strategy adopts this logic and at the same time reflects the Czech context by identifying general areas with high application potential (e.g., cybersecurity, sensors, materials research, post-quantum cryptography). Specifying priority sectors in too much detail at this stage could lead to premature narrowing of options and loss of synergies across the application sphere. More specific priorities will be set out in a follow-up implementation plan, which will be based on current technological trends, the results of an analysis of national capacities, and feedback from the application sector, and which will be regularly updated. This will ensure the necessary coordination of resources and openness to new innovation opportunities.

This includes funding to establish Quantum City, a world-class quantum science and technology hub at the University of Calgary in a strategic partnership with the University of Alberta and the University of Lethbridge.

premise_sentence: The quantum industry will also play an important role in improving diversity and inclusion.
premise_paragraph:
Quantum technologies present nearly endless opportunities. But these opportunities must serve the interests of Australian society and contribute to our national wellbeing. The public is increasingly aware of the ethical and social implications of new technologies, and we should not assume they will enthusiastically embrace quantum technologies.
By building principles for responsible development and use, quantum researchers and developers can develop technologies that align to Australian values and expectations and protect human rights. By adopting this approach, Australia can cement itself as a responsible technology developer and attract international companies and investors.
Standards for quantum technologies will give Australians confidence these technologies are being developed and deployed in a way that is trusted, secure and to their benefit. Technology standards and trusted measurement will help grow the sector by creating a competitive supplier market. This will give industry confidence to adopt new technologies and ensure global interoperability.
Standards will provide consistency and opportunities to Australian companies looking to develop or use this technology. With industry leading the way, Australia can have an active role in the global bodies that develop technology standards. This will help ensure that standards for quantum technologies support the growth of a vibrant and competitive marketplace by fostering interoperability, innovation, transparency and security.
The government will continue prioritising Australia’s representation in international quantum standards‑setting bodies and developing principles for responsible and inclusive development and use. This work is consistent with our international obligations and the principles of Australia’s international cyber and critical technology engagement. We will also ensure that developing and adopting these standards doesn’t unnecessarily inhibit research, development and commercialisation of quantum technologies.
To maximise the opportunities of quantum, we must push for greater inclusion and diversity. This includes increasing the participation of women, Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples and other underrepresented groups. The quantum community must engage with rural and regional Australia and connect with families, teachers and children. That way, the whole of Australia can understand and benefit from quantum technologies.
The quantum industry will also play an important role in improving diversity and inclusion. This includes reducing the gender pay gap and supporting women’s participation, particularly in senior positions.

The topic of quantum technologies is also included in the Czech Optical Cluster, founded in 2017, where optical quantum technologies represent one of the pillars. The cluster, which already has 43 members, connects the spheres of research, education, and industry and is also a way to establish international cooperation with industry clusters abroad. The quantum computer will have at least 24 qubits based on superconducting technology and connected in a "one-to-all" topology. It is therefore necessary to find financial resources to cover participation in European and non-European research programs where national co-financing is required, thereby placing the country among the advanced nations. Limited support from public and private funding, a tendency toward secrecy on the part of large companies and their unwillingness to cooperate with domestic research, an insufficient number of educational programs in the field of quantum technologies, and a low number of start-ups and spin-offs are among the main challenges for further development in this area.
Building a quantum sector in the Czech Republic is not only a matter of scientific progress, but also a fundamental opportunity to create a completely new industrial sector with high added value. A key factor for success will be the ability to effectively transfer technology from research institutions to industry and services, where quantum technologies can bring breakthrough solutions and new business opportunities. This transfer is necessary for Czechia to become not only a center of research excellence, but also a major player in the European and global market for quantum applications.
Quantum technologies are globally recognized as a breakthrough development with the potential to radically transform many strategically important areas. Developed countries around the world are therefore investing significant public funds in research and development in this area, with the key objectives being to gain a competitive advantage, ensure national security, and create a strong quantum ecosystem. These investments support not only basic and applied research, education, and talent development, but also the emergence of new technology start-ups and spin-offs and the commercialization of quantum innovations. Therefore, it is necessary to support a culture of founding deep-tech start-ups and spin-offs. Information on the measures taken, strategies, public and private resources, individual quantum opportunities and challenges that advanced countries such as the US52 , the Netherlands53 , Denmark54 , Sweden55, Australia56, the United Kingdom57 , Canada58 , Japan59 , andSouth Korea60 , can be found in the relevant national strategies of these countries.
In the Czech Republic, the potential for applying quantum technologies has been identified primarily in areas that are among the key innovation sectors of the national economy. These include, in particular, technologically advanced industries that use cutting-edge manufacturing technologies, automation, and digitization, where quantum simulations and calculations can significantly streamline the development of materials and manufacturing processes. They can also be used in the semiconductor industry and in the development of advanced chips. They also have applications in the fields of microelectronics, nanophotonics, and thin-film technologies. In cybersecurity
, quantum key distribution is a key tool for protecting sensitive data. High application potential is also expected in the healthcare and pharmaceutical research sectors, particularly in the field of biomolecule modeling and accelerating drug development, as well as in the energy sector, for example in network optimization or research into new types of batteries. Significant opportunities for the use of quantum technologies are also opening up in transportation, logistics, finance, and the defense industry.
However, quantum technologies represent a revolutionary and constantly evolving field whose full application potential is only just beginning to be discovered. Many of the future applications—as was the case with semiconductors and the internet in the past—cannot be accurately predicted today, and overly narrow sector-specific specifications at this stage could limit flexibility and the ability to respond quickly to new opportunities. That is why the Quantum Europe Strategy (2025) does not define specific application sectors in detail, but works with an open framework that allows Member States to dynamically develop quantum technologies according to current technological developments, research capacities, and industrial demand. The national strategy adopts this logic and at the same time reflects the Czech context by identifying general areas with high application potential (e.g., cybersecurity, sensors, materials research, post-quantum cryptography). Specifying priority sectors in too much detail at this stage could lead to premature narrowing of options and loss of synergies across the application sphere. More specific priorities will be set out in a follow-up implementation plan, which will be based on current technological trends, the results of an analysis of national capacities, and feedback from the application sector, and which will be regularly updated. This will ensure the necessary coordination of resources and openness to new innovation opportunities.

premise_sentence: The thermophysical properties of molecular hydrogen depend critically on the spin state of the molecule.
premise_paragraph:
Australia’s quantum opportunity is immense. Australia is recognised as a global quantum leader, with some of the best minds in quantum research and applied technology. The government is taking action to grow the tech sector – including quantum technologies – to improve our economy’s long‑term performance and the prosperity of all Australians.
The technology sector is already Australia’s third‑largest industry, (<https://techcouncil.com.au/wp-content/uploads/2022/03/2022-Tech-Jobs-Opportunity-report.pdf>)employing 1 in 16 workers and contributing (<https://techcouncil.com.au/wp-content/uploads/2021/08/TCA-Tech-sectors-economic-contribution-full-res.pdf>)$167 billion to our economy every year, or 8.5% of GDP. The sector is growing rapidly and is forecast to contribute $244 billion a year by 2031.
Quantum capabilities will amplify the growth of this sector and lead to entirely new kinds of technologies in the longer term. Australian quantum companies already attract significant venture capital investment compared to our international competitors, capturing a (<https://techcouncil.com.au/wp-content/uploads/2022/08/Turning-Australia-into-a-regional-tech-hub_Report-2022.pdf>)3.6% share of global venture capital for quantum from 2017 to 2021.
Case study: Quantum supporting other economic sectors
Quantum science can be applied to improve our understanding of industrial processes.
An Australian start‑up, Jovian Tech, is building process instrumentation to measure the spin‑isomer ratio in hydrogen molecules. The thermophysical properties of molecular hydrogen depend critically on the spin state of the molecule.
Chemical engineers can use information from Jovian Tech to optimise hydrogen production plant operations and lower costs in the hydrogen economy.
The importance of quantum technologies extends beyond economics, jobs, and applications for business and industry. In time, quantum technologies will be an important national capability supporting our security and our way of life. Ensuring Australia has a sovereign quantum capability will let us keep pace with the latest advances and shape the evolving technology landscape in our national interest.
Realising the potential of quantum technologies will have risks and challenges. The main challenges facing Australia’s quantum sector have been identified and are addressed through this strategy (see ‘Quantum opportunities and challenges’, below).
Other challenges that need to be carefully managed include:
- • economic headwinds
- • a tightening global investment climate
- • uncertainty over development timelines and which technologies will be successful.
International competition will also create uncertainty and opportunities. Countries across the globe are investing billions into quantum technologies, and the Australian industry needs support to keep its leading position. Australia has already invested hundreds of millions of dollars in establishing deep technical skills. It is now time to build on and increase these efforts while building in resilience to global changes.
Case study: Addressing transportation challenges
In 2020 Transport for NSW approached (<https://q-ctrl.com>)Q‑CTRL with an interest in understanding how they – as an organisation with heavy computational challenges – could put quantum computing to work for them. Many problems in managing transport services are computationally challenging – from timetabling of intersecting modes of transport to meet known patterns of demand through to dynamically routing vehicles to meet changing traffic patterns. These problems quickly grow intractable, even for advanced computing tools, as the number of ‘interacting’ modes of transport, vehicles, connection points and stops grows.
Q‑CTRL took on the challenge and has focused on getting Transport for NSW quantum ready via a combination of quantum professional services and quantum control infrastructure software development. Q‑CTRL worked with Transport for NSW to improve the performance of quantum algorithms relevant to transport optimisation problems and charted a path to quantum advantage.
Q‑CTRL identified a (<https://youtu.be/chYBOL6ylJk>)new hardware‑efficient algorithmic implementation which enables modelling of transport networks on near‑term quantum computers using fewer computational resources than otherwise expected. The team built a (<https://docs.q-ctrl.com/boulder-opal/user-guides/how-to-simulate-multi-qubit-circuits-in-quantum-computing>)custom circuit simulator to simulate multiqubit algorithms subject to the real noise endemic to near‑term quantum computer hardware, and tested their prototype solutions on real cloud‑accessible machines. The results demonstrated that the target mobility‑as‑a‑service problem could really be efficiently solved by a quantum computer, even in the presence of imperfections.
Having demonstrated a real quantum advantage in large‑scale network optimisation looks to be possible, Q‑CTRL is currently building a custom software package for Transport for NSW. This will deliver the ability to optimize the execution of quantum algorithms for mobility‑as‑a‑service problems to end users with expertise in transport analytics and data science.

premise_sentence: Australian researchers, start‑ups and quantum businesses need advanced infrastructure, materials and tools to build a thriving quantum ecosystem across research, development and manufacturing.
premise_paragraph:
Australian researchers, start‑ups and quantum businesses need advanced infrastructure, materials and tools to build a thriving quantum ecosystem across research, development and manufacturing.
Infrastructure and material needs differ across the quantum sector. For example:
- • accessing existing quantum computing capabilities could help develop use cases for quantum technologies
- • growing and effectively using existing manufacturing facilities will help translate research into applications
- • access to flexible fabrication plants may help quantum researchers and start‑ups access cheaper components and grow.
Reliable access to resilient and trusted domestic and international supply chains is also essential for a successful Australian quantum sector. Quantum technologies use mass‑produced components, such as semiconductors, that are in high demand across the world. They also need bespoke components that are available from single sources or specialist manufacturers.
The Australian quantum industry relies on complex global supply chains for key materials and components. These complex global supply chains are vulnerable to disruption, resulting in unpredictable availability and costs. Australia could analyse future supply chain needs and identify areas where it can become a world leader, helping grow ongoing supply in the future.

premise_sentence: In 2020, Masaryk University, CTU, and Brno University of Technology established a registered institute for cybersecurity, CyberSecurity Hub, z.ú.
premise_paragraph:
In 2016, t h e Czech Republic launched the National Initiative for Quantum Technologies, which was launched in connection with the preparation of the European EU Quantum Flagship initiative and aims to promote and support the development of quantum technologies. The topic of quantum technologies is also included in the Czech Optical Cluster, founded in 2017, where optical quantum technologies represent one of the pillars. The cluster, which already has 43 members, connects the spheres of research, education, and industry and is also a way to establish international cooperation with industry clusters abroad. Thanks to the link between the further development of semiconductor technologies and quantum technologies, the topic of quantum technologies is also emphasized on the platform of the Czech National Semiconductor Cluster.
Research teams from the Czech Republic are actively involved in selected European research programs and initiatives in the field of quantum technologies. For example, as part of EuroHPC JU, a VQL quantum computer will be installed in 2025 at the IT4Innovations VŠB-TUO national supercomputing center. It is funded by the EU and the international LUMI-Q consortium, which brings together partners from nine EU countries. The quantum computer will have at least 24 qubits based on superconducting technology and connected in a "one-to-all" topology. For the implementation of hybrid quantum-classical tasks, it will be integrated with the EuroHPC Karolina supercomputer operated at the IT4Innovations center. In 2025, VŠB-TUO became a member of the European association QuIC (Quantum Industry Consortium), which seeks to accelerate the development of quantum technologies and strengthen Europe's role as a global leader in this dynamically developing field.
Through the EuroQCI initiative, the Czech Republic has joined research leading to the construction of a secure quantum communication backbone network in the EU. This project is considered the most extensive project in the field of quantum communication to date and represents the culmination of decades of research, development, and testing. In the Czech Republic, it is coordinated by CyberSecurity Hub, z.ú. and a consortium of Czech universities is working on it.
On January 29, 2024, the Czech Technical University in Prague (CTU) connected to IBM's quantum computer (400 qubits) and signed a cooperation agreement in the field of quantum
technology between other universities and research organizations. CTU also cooperates with seventeen other partners from EU universities and companies on the European Photonic Quantum Computer (Epique) project funded by the European Commission. The Department of Coherent Optics of the Institute of Scientific Instruments (ÚPT) of the Czech Academy of Sciences is also involved in a number of consortium projects supporting both its own development of quantum ion clocks within the European metrology community and, above all, the implementation and expansion of a network of coherent transmission lines for the distribution of precise time derived from these clocks. ÚPT is very actively involved in the research and development of its own technology and is at the forefront of European efforts in this field.
Another important project is the Horizon Europe program project Center for Artificial Intelligence and Quantum Computing in Systems Brain Research (CLARA), co-financed by the Jan Amos Komenský Operational Program (OP JAK), which aims to build an interdisciplinary center of excellence focused on the use of artificial intelligence and quantum computing for research into neurodegenerative diseases. As part of this center, a testbed will be created, which will include quantum computers at VŠB-TUO and the LRZ center in Bavaria and, together with the new HPCQC-AI computing cluster, will enable the implementation of hybrid quantum-classical computations.
In the field of quantum technologies, two large consortium projects were supported under the Excellent Research call of the OP JAK program – QEENTEC led by the Institute of Scientific Instruments of the Czech Academy of Sciences and bringing together the Institute of Photonics and Electronics, the Jaroslav Heyrovský Institute of Physical Chemistry, Palacký University in Olomouc, and CESNET; and the QM4ST consortium project led by the University of West Bohemia in Plzeň, bringing together Charles University, Masaryk University, and the Brno University of Technology. The first project aims to develop hybrid quantum gates for quantum computers. The second project, QM4ST, focuses on research and development, particularly in the modeling and design of new materials, but also on the control of processes that are part of new devices. This creates an opportunity for Czechia to join the world's leading players in the field of quantum chip and computer research and development and thus secure a position in their commercialization, if not in the form of their final production, then at least in the role of suppliers of certain key components.
The Czech Republic is one of the European countries actively involved in the European quantum ecosystem. Cutting-edge research activities are carried out in the fields of quantum computing, quantum communication, quantum sensors, metrology, and quantum materials. Research teams are mainly concentrated at universities and institutes of the Czech Academy of Sciences across the Czech Republic (Figure 2). Important research centers include Palacký University in Olomouc, particularly the Department of Optics at the Faculty of Science, the joint optics laboratory of Palacký University and the Institute of Physics of the Czech Academy of Sciences, and the Czech Institute of Research and Advanced Technologies. Other institutions include the Czech Technical University, particularly the Department of Physics and the Department of Physical Electronics of the Faculty of Nuclear and Physical Engineering, the Faculty of Electrical Engineering, and the Faculty of Biomedical Engineering. At Charles University, it is primarily the Faculty of Mathematics and Physics. At the Czech Academy of Sciences, it is primarily the Institute of Instrumentation, the Institute of Physics, the Institute of Organic Chemistry and Biochemistry, the J. Heyrovský Institute of Physical Chemistry, the Institute of Mathematics, and the Institute of Computer Science. At Masaryk University, it is primarily the Faculty of Science and the Faculty of Informatics. At Mendel University in Brno, it is primarily the Institute of Chemistry and Biochemistry. At the University of West Bohemia in Pilsen, it is primarily the University Institute of New Technologies – Research Center and the Department of Physics of the Faculty of Applied Sciences. At the Brno University of Technology, it is mainly the Faculty of Electrical Engineering and Communication Technologies (Quantum Security Laboratory) and the Faculty of Information Technology. At VŠB-TUO, it is mainly the IT4Innovations National Supercomputing Center and the Faculty of Electrical Engineering and Computer Science. In 2020, Masaryk University, CTU, and Brno University of Technology established a registered institute for cybersecurity, CyberSecurity Hub, z.ú. Other important institutions include CEITEC, ELI ERIC, and the CESNET interest group, whose members include public and state universities and the Czech Academy of Sciences. ČD Telematika also cooperates in the development and implementation of secure quantum communication and infrastructure in particular.
Figure 2: Map of Czech research activities in the field of quantum technologies
In the field of education, there are several educational programs currently being prepared for dozens of students at all levels.
The Czech Technical University in Prague offers a complete university education (Bachelor's, Master's, Ph.D.) in quantum technologies, including participation in the DigiQ project focused on the development of educational programs in the field of quantum technologies within the European education area. In addition, a master's program in quantum informatics was launched here in 2025.
Palacký University in Olomouc offers a wide range of fields and specializations covering many areas of modern quantum optics, the interaction of radiation with atoms, ions, mechanical oscillators, solid-state or superconducting circuits, and their applications in quantum technologies. Charles University teaches a wide range of subjects in the field of quantum sciences in condensed and solid materials, nonlinear optics, low-temperature physics, and a number of related fields. Masaryk University teaches a course in quantum information processing. At VŠB-TUO, subjects such as Introduction to Quantum Computing, Quantum Error Correction, Quantum Computer Programming, and Quantum Algorithms are taught. In addition, a master's program focused on high-performance computing, quantum computing, and artificial intelligence is in preparation, with a planned start date in 2026. The University of Chemistry and Technology in Prague teaches courses in Quantum Chemistry and Quantum Mechanics for Materials Engineering. The University of West Bohemia in Pilsen is preparing a doctoral program in Physics for Quantum Materials.
If we compare the Czech Republic with other countries in all contexts, such as the US, Australia, the UK, the Netherlands, Ireland, Denmark, or Spain, we see an opportunity to support the development of quantum technologies, especially in terms of targeting effective application. Compared to developed countries, the Czech Republic still lags behind in its ability to provide targeted support for quantum science, transfer know-how and technologies from the world of quantum science and research to the world of practice, and in effective and systematic long-term cooperation between universities, institutes of the Czech Academy of Sciences, and the private sector. This situation can be improved by increasing the number of experts capable of working with quantum technologies in companies. This underscores the fundamental importance of education and retraining in this field.
There are few new start-ups or university or academic spin-offs in the Czech Republic that focus on the application of quantum technologies. There are investment funds that focus, among other things, on investments in quantum technologies, such as Tensor Ventures and Presto Ventures. The first companies are also emerging that are attempting to use and create quantum algorithms for their own use. A great opportunity is that there are a number of multinational companies operating in the Czech Republic that are intensively engaged in the development of quantum technologies, primarily in the areas of quantum computers, quantum communication, and quantum sensors and materials. The presence of multinational companies with local branches or research centers that support the development of quantum technologies, for example in the areas of quantum computers, communications, sensors, and materials, creates opportunities for cooperation with Czech entities. Companies such as Microsoft, AWS, Google, and IBM are developing global initiatives that may also be an opportunity for Czech industry and academia. Opportunities for cooperation are also provided by a number of European companies operating in the field of quantum technologies, such as IQM, Pasqal, Quandela, Qilimanjaro, Terra Quantum, ParityQC, and others.
Thanks to its integration into the European Research Area and the presence of renowned research institutions and experts, Czechia has a strong starting position for involvement in the European and global quantum ecosystem. premise_paragraph:
These immediate actions address the challenges and opportunities identified in this strategy by:
- • helping academia, industry and the community work together to solve challenges by using quantum capabilities
- • growing Australia’s quantum ecosystem by improving coordination and collaboration between research and industry. This will identify the uses that will increase quantum uptake and investment
- • increasing investment by building on local expertise and sovereign capability to grow the economy, increase productivity and create jobs.
National Reconstruction Fund
The Australian Government is establishing the $15 billion (<https://www.industry.gov.au/news/national-reconstruction-fund-diversifying-and-transforming-australias-industry-and-economy>)National Reconstruction Fund (NRF) to support, diversify and transform Australia’s industry. This will secure our future prosperity and drive sustainable economic growth.
The NRF will target projects and investments that help Australia capture new, high‑value market opportunities. This will help our businesses grow and succeed in the economy of tomorrow. The NRF will provide finance (including loans, guarantees and equity) to drive investments that add value and develop capability in 7 priority areas. The NRF will partner with industry to unlock private sector investment to create sustainable industries and secure, well‑paid jobs.
The NRF will be an independent financier that operates commercially to deliver a positive rate of return. It will be governed by a board who will make independent investment decisions guided by an investment mandate.
One of the NRF’s priority areas is enabling capabilities. It includes $1 billion of finance to grow advanced manufacturing and support businesses to innovate and move up the technological ladder.
A further $1 billion will be used to grow critical technologies. This will support home‑grown innovation and build industry capability in areas like artificial intelligence, quantum computing, robotics and software development. The NRF will commence operation in 2023.
Case study: Quantum supporting medical testing
Quantum scientists at the University of Melbourne, in collaboration with the Florey Institute of Neuroscience and Mental Health, are developing FeBI (ferritin bound iron) diagnostic technology using a patented quantum sensing technology to detect iron loading within serum ferritin.
This new technology contrasts with current tests which are confounded by inflammation due to their reliance on the ferritin protein levels rather than iron. A working prototype has been demonstrated to be functional on laboratory ferritin samples and the team is currently applying for funding and investment for clinical validation and commercial development.

premise_sentence: Therefore, there is a need for knowledge sharing and guidance on the possibilities of quantum technology for potential end users, so that more people can benefit from quantum technologies.
premise_paragraph:
Denmark's strong position in quantum research must benefit both business and society. Our strong research position must be developed to also become a strong business position, which supports Denmark's long-term competitiveness.
Therefore, the government will actively strengthen the emerging Danish ecosystem for applied quantum technology, so that the Danish research environments and start-ups in the quantum field as well as in the established business community with an interest in and potential for using quantum technologies can become even closer connected.
Quantum technology is still in the early maturity stage, and the companies in the quantum field are mainly spin-outs from universities. In order to speed up the commercialisation of quantum technology, there is a need to improve access to facilities and funding, as well as support the development of and access to the right skills. An important part of the work is to increase awareness of the technology's
potential applications. The Boston Consulting Group estimates that, of the 450-850 billion dollars, which generally applicable and faulttolerant quantum computers can generate in value, around 80 percent is expected to come from end users within e.g. life science and financial service companies. Therefore, there is a need for knowledge sharing and guidance on the possibilities of quantum technology for potential end users, so that more people can benefit from quantum technologies.
Reaping the gains requires flexibility and risk tolerance on the behalf of the companies, but also for employees to have the right skills and knowledge. Denmark has a strong higher education system in quantum science, particularly in physics, chemistry, nanoscience, photonics, mathematics and computer science. But there is a need to secure talent development and meet an increasing need for interdisciplinary knowledge and skills to be successful with quantum technology – at both educational and research institutions and in the business world.
Examples of quantum technology consultants and users
- • Danske Bank and Energinet – participate in field experiments with quantum communication
- • Novo Nordisk – is investigating quantum simulation and computing for drug discovery
- • TDC NET – has used quantum algorithms for route optimization for service technicians
- • KPMG – has located its global quantum hub in Denmark and focuses on applied quantum technology via advisory and consulting services

premise_sentence: However, edge technology does not yet have the computational ability necessary to handle complex algorithms, limiting the application of mainstream edge computing devices for heavy‑duty computing.
premise_paragraph:
Autonomous vehicles, robotics, and smartphones are just 3 examples of edge computing devices that have become mainstream technologies. However, edge technology does not yet have the computational ability necessary to handle complex algorithms, limiting the application of mainstream edge computing devices for heavy‑duty computing.
Quantum computing could change that. As part of the Quantum Pioneers Program, the (<https://pawsey.org.au/>)Pawsey (<https://pawsey.org.au/>)Supercomputing Research Centre partnered with (<https://quantumbrilliance.com/>)Quantum Brilliance to enable industry and research teams to explore the possibilities of edge quantum computing. One example of this is accelerating speech transcription at the edge.
Quantum Brilliance worked with an industry partner (<https://trellisdata.com/>)Trellis to develop the Quantum Decoder – a hybrid quantum – classical application that seeks to improve speech transcription algorithms. The Quantum Decoder will replace the ‘beam search’ decoder algorithms that are deployed in many everyday applications like vehicles and smartphones. Classical decoder algorithms truncate input data to provide results quickly, but some input information is lost, reducing accuracy.
The Quantum Decoder promises to be both fast and accurate. It uses a quantum computer to decode the input signal in a reasonable timeframe with no loss of information. This provides better accuracy at the same computational speed as similar‑sized classical systems. The Quantum Decoder could be generalised to any signal processing problem, not just speech transcription. This means it has the potential to impact many industries, autonomous vehicles, robotics, smartphones, satellite image processing and manufacturing optimisation.
Autonomous vehicles and robotics are examples of edge computing devices. Credit: Quantum Brilliance
The recently announced ARC Centre of Excellence in Quantum Biotechnology will help Australia stay at the forefront of developing quantum technologies to transform our understanding of life. Credit: Getty Images
Case study: Value chain for domestic supply and manufacturing
(<https://sqc.com.au/>)Silicon Quantum Computing (SQC) has worked with Australian industry to help build an end‑to‑end quantum value chain.
To feed the manufacturing underway, SQC secures raw materials from a local provider Silex Systems. With the support of the Department of Industry, Science and Resources’ CRC program, Silex expanded its operations to include the production of isotopically pure silicon at its facilities in Lucas Heights, NSW.
Down‑stream of the hardware, SQC procures services from quantum software company Aqacia, which helps develop machine learning tools focused on accelerating quantum computing chip development and insights. While SQC is one of their local ‘anchors’, the digital nature of the company means that Aqacia is able to provide services to, and earn revenue from, all over the world.
At the other end of the local value chain, SQC has had a long‑standing relationship with the Commonwealth Bank and Telstra, its co‑development partners who recognised early the transformational nature of quantum computing to their business. As the technology continues to mature, these essential partnerships have informed the company of the valuable use cases and accelerated the development of the full quantum computing stack in SQC to meet these requirements.
Silicon Quantum Computing laboratory at UNSW Sydney. Credit: Silicon Quantum Computing
