premise_paragraph:
This publication is Part 1 of a National Strategy for Quantum Technology. In the autumn of 2023, the government will present the last part of the strategy with a focus on the entire ecosystem and, thus, support both the development, marketing and use of quantum technology in Denmark.
Part 1 has been prepared by the Ministry of Higher Education and Science in close dialogue with the other parties in the interministerial quantum secretariat consisting of the Ministry for Industry, Business and Financial Affairs, the Ministry of Foreign Affairs, the Ministry of Defence and the Ministry of Digital Government and Gender Equality.
Important input to the strategy is the mapping of research in the quantum area, which the Danish Agency for Higher Education and Science carried out in the spring of 2022. The mapping uncovers, among other things, the scope and nature of quantum research in Denmark, strengths and weaknesses, the universities' interaction with companies, marketing of research, building of skills and Denmark's international cooperation in the field. The mapping is based on the compilation of data from a number of sub-analyses, including a survey among universities, research institutions, organizations and private and public research funding foundations.
As an introduction to the strategy work, the Ministry of Higher Education and Science has held a round table discussion in June 2022 for universities, research institutions, organizations, and private research funding foundations. The Ministry of Higher Education and Science has also held a number of bilateral dialogue meetings with the actors for gathering input for the strategy work.
In particular, the following reports have contributed to the strategy's knowledge base:
- • Uddannelses- og Forskningsstyrelsen (2022): Kortlægning af forskning på kvanteområdet og samspillet mellem universiteter og virksomheder
- • Erhvervsstyrelsen (2022): Kortlægning af økosystemet for kvanteteknologi i dansk erhvervsliv
- • Danish Quantum Community (2022): Danish Quantum Agenda 2022
- • Danish Quantum Community (2022): Quantum-Related Cybersecurity in Denmark
- • KPMG (2020): Quantum technology in Denmark – The case for Danish investment in quantum technology
- • EU-Kommissionen (2020): Strategic Research Agenda – Quantum Flagship

premise_sentence:
On January 29, 2024, the Czech Technical University in Prague (CTU) connected to IBM's quantum computer (400 qubits) and signed a cooperation agreement in the field of quantum
technology between other universities and research organizations.
premise_paragraph:
In 2016, t h e Czech Republic launched the National Initiative for Quantum Technologies, which was launched in connection with the preparation of the European EU Quantum Flagship initiative and aims to promote and support the development of quantum technologies. The topic of quantum technologies is also included in the Czech Optical Cluster, founded in 2017, where optical quantum technologies represent one of the pillars. The cluster, which already has 43 members, connects the spheres of research, education, and industry and is also a way to establish international cooperation with industry clusters abroad. Thanks to the link between the further development of semiconductor technologies and quantum technologies, the topic of quantum technologies is also emphasized on the platform of the Czech National Semiconductor Cluster.
Research teams from the Czech Republic are actively involved in selected European research programs and initiatives in the field of quantum technologies. For example, as part of EuroHPC JU, a VQL quantum computer will be installed in 2025 at the IT4Innovations VŠB-TUO national supercomputing center. It is funded by the EU and the international LUMI-Q consortium, which brings together partners from nine EU countries. The quantum computer will have at least 24 qubits based on superconducting technology and connected in a "one-to-all" topology. For the implementation of hybrid quantum-classical tasks, it will be integrated with the EuroHPC Karolina supercomputer operated at the IT4Innovations center. In 2025, VŠB-TUO became a member of the European association QuIC (Quantum Industry Consortium), which seeks to accelerate the development of quantum technologies and strengthen Europe's role as a global leader in this dynamically developing field.
Through the EuroQCI initiative, the Czech Republic has joined research leading to the construction of a secure quantum communication backbone network in the EU. This project is considered the most extensive project in the field of quantum communication to date and represents the culmination of decades of research, development, and testing. In the Czech Republic, it is coordinated by CyberSecurity Hub, z.ú. and a consortium of Czech universities is working on it.
On January 29, 2024, the Czech Technical University in Prague (CTU) connected to IBM's quantum computer (400 qubits) and signed a cooperation agreement in the field of quantum
technology between other universities and research organizations. CTU also cooperates with seventeen other partners from EU universities and companies on the European Photonic Quantum Computer (Epique) project funded by the European Commission. The Department of Coherent Optics of the Institute of Scientific Instruments (ÚPT) of the Czech Academy of Sciences is also involved in a number of consortium projects supporting both its own development of quantum ion clocks within the European metrology community and, above all, the implementation and expansion of a network of coherent transmission lines for the distribution of precise time derived from these clocks. ÚPT is very actively involved in the research and development of its own technology and is at the forefront of European efforts in this field.
Another important project is the Horizon Europe program project Center for Artificial Intelligence and Quantum Computing in Systems Brain Research (CLARA), co-financed by the Jan Amos Komenský Operational Program (OP JAK), which aims to build an interdisciplinary center of excellence focused on the use of artificial intelligence and quantum computing for research into neurodegenerative diseases. As part of this center, a testbed will be created, which will include quantum computers at VŠB-TUO and the LRZ center in Bavaria and, together with the new HPCQC-AI computing cluster, will enable the implementation of hybrid quantum-classical computations.
In the field of quantum technologies, two large consortium projects were supported under the Excellent Research call of the OP JAK program – QEENTEC led by the Institute of Scientific Instruments of the Czech Academy of Sciences and bringing together the Institute of Photonics and Electronics, the Jaroslav Heyrovský Institute of Physical Chemistry, Palacký University in Olomouc, and CESNET; and the QM4ST consortium project led by the University of West Bohemia in Plzeň, bringing together Charles University, Masaryk University, and the Brno University of Technology. The first project aims to develop hybrid quantum gates for quantum computers. The second project, QM4ST, focuses on research and development, particularly in the modeling and design of new materials, but also on the control of processes that are part of new devices. This creates an opportunity for Czechia to join the world's leading players in the field of quantum chip and computer research and development and thus secure a position in their commercialization, if not in the form of their final production, then at least in the role of suppliers of certain key components.
The Czech Republic is one of the European countries actively involved in the European quantum ecosystem. Cutting-edge research activities are carried out in the fields of quantum computing, quantum communication, quantum sensors, metrology, and quantum materials. Research teams are mainly concentrated at universities and institutes of the Czech Academy of Sciences across the Czech Republic (Figure 2). Important research centers include Palacký University in Olomouc, particularly the Department of Optics at the Faculty of Science, the joint optics laboratory of Palacký University and the Institute of Physics of the Czech Academy of Sciences, and the Czech Institute of Research and Advanced Technologies. Other institutions include the Czech Technical University, particularly the Department of Physics and the Department of Physical Electronics of the Faculty of Nuclear and Physical Engineering, the Faculty of Electrical Engineering, and the Faculty of Biomedical Engineering. At Charles University, it is primarily the Faculty of Mathematics and Physics. At the Czech Academy of Sciences, it is primarily the Institute of Instrumentation, the Institute of Physics, the Institute of Organic Chemistry and Biochemistry, the J. Heyrovský Institute of Physical Chemistry, the Institute of Mathematics, and the Institute of Computer Science. At Masaryk University, it is primarily the Faculty of Science and the Faculty of Informatics. At Mendel University in Brno, it is primarily the Institute of Chemistry and Biochemistry. At the University of West Bohemia in Pilsen, it is primarily the University Institute of New Technologies – Research Center and the Department of Physics of the Faculty of Applied Sciences. At the Brno University of Technology, it is mainly the Faculty of Electrical Engineering and Communication Technologies (Quantum Security Laboratory) and the Faculty of Information Technology. At VŠB-TUO, it is mainly the IT4Innovations National Supercomputing Center and the Faculty of Electrical Engineering and Computer Science. In 2020, Masaryk University, CTU, and Brno University of Technology established a registered institute for cybersecurity, CyberSecurity Hub, z.ú. Other important institutions include CEITEC, ELI ERIC, and the CESNET interest group, whose members include public and state universities and the Czech Academy of Sciences. ČD Telematika also cooperates in the development and implementation of secure quantum communication and infrastructure in particular.
Figure 2: Map of Czech research activities in the field of quantum technologies
In the field of education, there are several educational programs currently being prepared for dozens of students at all levels.
The Czech Technical University in Prague offers a complete university education (Bachelor's, Master's, Ph.D.) in quantum technologies, including participation in the DigiQ project focused on the development of educational programs in the field of quantum technologies within the European education area. In addition, a master's program in quantum informatics was launched here in 2025.
Palacký University in Olomouc offers a wide range of fields and specializations covering many areas of modern quantum optics, the interaction of radiation with atoms, ions, mechanical oscillators, solid-state or superconducting circuits, and their applications in quantum technologies. Charles University teaches a wide range of subjects in the field of quantum sciences in condensed and solid materials, nonlinear optics, low-temperature physics, and a number of related fields. Masaryk University teaches a course in quantum information processing. At VŠB-TUO, subjects such as Introduction to Quantum Computing, Quantum Error Correction, Quantum Computer Programming, and Quantum Algorithms are taught. In addition, a master's program focused on high-performance computing, quantum computing, and artificial intelligence is in preparation, with a planned start date in 2026. The University of Chemistry and Technology in Prague teaches courses in Quantum Chemistry and Quantum Mechanics for Materials Engineering. The University of West Bohemia in Pilsen is preparing a doctoral program in Physics for Quantum Materials.
If we compare the Czech Republic with other countries in all contexts, such as the US, Australia, the UK, the Netherlands, Ireland, Denmark, or Spain, we see an opportunity to support the development of quantum technologies, especially in terms of targeting effective application. Compared to developed countries, the Czech Republic still lags behind in its ability to provide targeted support for quantum science, transfer know-how and technologies from the world of quantum science and research to the world of practice, and in effective and systematic long-term cooperation between universities, institutes of the Czech Academy of Sciences, and the private sector. This situation can be improved by increasing the number of experts capable of working with quantum technologies in companies. This underscores the fundamental importance of education and retraining in this field.
There are few new start-ups or university or academic spin-offs in the Czech Republic that focus on the application of quantum technologies. There are investment funds that focus, among other things, on investments in quantum technologies, such as Tensor Ventures and Presto Ventures. The first companies are also emerging that are attempting to use and create quantum algorithms for their own use. A great opportunity is that there are a number of multinational companies operating in the Czech Republic that are intensively engaged in the development of quantum technologies, primarily in the areas of quantum computers, quantum communication, and quantum sensors and materials. The presence of multinational companies with local branches or research centers that support the development of quantum technologies, for example in the areas of quantum computers, communications, sensors, and materials, creates opportunities for cooperation with Czech entities. Companies such as Microsoft, AWS, Google, and IBM are developing global initiatives that may also be an opportunity for Czech industry and academia. Opportunities for cooperation are also provided by a number of European companies operating in the field of quantum technologies, such as IQM, Pasqal, Quandela, Qilimanjaro, Terra Quantum, ParityQC, and others.
Thanks to its integration into the European Research Area and the presence of renowned research institutions and experts, Czechia has a strong starting position for involvement in the European and global quantum ecosystem. The Czech company Amires s.r.o. administers the important European projects Qu-test and Qu-pilot, which focus on making research capacities available to companies involved in quantum technologies. The opportunity also creates the possibility for Czech entities to participate in "quantum chips pilot lines." This integration offers research institutions and domestic companies the chance to be at the forefront of new technologies and thus become part of the emerging market for quantum applications. It is therefore necessary to find financial resources to cover participation in European and non-European research programs where national co-financing is required, thereby placing the country among the advanced nations. Limited support from public and private funding, a tendency toward secrecy on the part of large companies and their unwillingness to cooperate with domestic research, an insufficient number of educational programs in the field of quantum technologies, and a low number of start-ups and spin-offs are among the main challenges for further development in this area.
Building a quantum sector in the Czech Republic is not only a matter of scientific progress, but also a fundamental opportunity to create a completely new industrial sector with high added value. A key factor for success will be the ability to effectively transfer technology from research institutions to industry and services, where quantum technologies can bring breakthrough solutions and new business opportunities. This transfer is necessary for Czechia to become not only a center of research excellence, but also a major player in the European and global market for quantum applications.
Quantum technologies are globally recognized as a breakthrough development with the potential to radically transform many strategically important areas. Developed countries around the world are therefore investing significant public funds in research and development in this area, with the key objectives being to gain a competitive advantage, ensure national security, and create a strong quantum ecosystem. These investments support not only basic and applied research, education, and talent development, but also the emergence of new technology start-ups and spin-offs and the commercialization of quantum innovations. Therefore, it is necessary to support a culture of founding deep-tech start-ups and spin-offs. Information on the measures taken, strategies, public and private resources, individual quantum opportunities and challenges that advanced countries such as the US52 , the Netherlands53 , Denmark54 , Sweden55, Australia56, the United Kingdom57 , Canada58 , Japan59 , andSouth Korea60 , can be found in the relevant national strategies of these countries.
In the Czech Republic, the potential for applying quantum technologies has been identified primarily in areas that are among the key innovation sectors of the national economy. These include, in particular, technologically advanced industries that use cutting-edge manufacturing technologies, automation, and digitization, where quantum simulations and calculations can significantly streamline the development of materials and manufacturing processes. They can also be used in the semiconductor industry and in the development of advanced chips. They also have applications in the fields of microelectronics, nanophotonics, and thin-film technologies. In cybersecurity
, quantum key distribution is a key tool for protecting sensitive data. High application potential is also expected in the healthcare and pharmaceutical research sectors, particularly in the field of biomolecule modeling and accelerating drug development, as well as in the energy sector, for example in network optimization or research into new types of batteries. Significant opportunities for the use of quantum technologies are also opening up in transportation, logistics, finance, and the defense industry.
However, quantum technologies represent a revolutionary and constantly evolving field whose full application potential is only just beginning to be discovered. Many of the future applications—as was the case with semiconductors and the internet in the past—cannot be accurately predicted today, and overly narrow sector-specific specifications at this stage could limit flexibility and the ability to respond quickly to new opportunities. That is why the Quantum Europe Strategy (2025) does not define specific application sectors in detail, but works with an open framework that allows Member States to dynamically develop quantum technologies according to current technological developments, research capacities, and industrial demand. The national strategy adopts this logic and at the same time reflects the Czech context by identifying general areas with high application potential (e.g., cybersecurity, sensors, materials research, post-quantum cryptography). Specifying priority sectors in too much detail at this stage could lead to premature narrowing of options and loss of synergies across the application sphere. More specific priorities will be set out in a follow-up implementation plan, which will be based on current technological trends, the results of an analysis of national capacities, and feedback from the application sector, and which will be regularly updated. This will ensure the necessary coordination of resources and openness to new innovation opportunities.

Current NCRIS projects that could improve access for quantum researchers include the following:
- •
(<https://www.anff.org.au/>)Australian National Fabrication Facility, which provides open access micro‑ and nanofabrication for Australian researchers in 8 nodes across Queensland, NSW, Victoria, South Australia and Western Australia.
premise_paragraph:
The Australian Government has invested in a range of research infrastructure, some of which could be used to improve access for quantum researchers and businesses.
The (<https://www.education.gov.au/ncris>)National Collaborative Research Infrastructure Strategy (NCRIS) is maintaining Australia’s position as a global research leader by ensuring researchers can access cutting‑edge infrastructure. Continuing to invest in NCRIS, guided by the 2021 National Research Infrastructure Roadmap, will ensure this infrastructure evolves to meet the needs of quantum researchers.
Current NCRIS projects that could improve access for quantum researchers include the following:
- •
(<https://www.anff.org.au/>)Australian National Fabrication Facility, which provides open access micro‑ and nanofabrication for Australian researchers in 8 nodes across Queensland, NSW, Victoria, South Australia and Western Australia. Each node has expertise in a specific area, such as advanced materials, bionanotechnology applications, or nanoelectronics and photonics. This can help researchers develop new products and improve current production methods.
- •
(<https://www.arin.org.au/program/63bbb488f0b0a911525c5b6c3683b02c>)Heavy Ion Accelerators, which provides ion implantation and ion beam analysis services to national and international researchers. This includes researchers working closely with industry in quantum computing, photovoltaics, nano‑photonics and microelectronics.
- •
(<https://micro.org.au/>)Microscopy Australia, which provides infrastructure that has helped investigate nanowires made from a new material called indium arsenide.
- • The (<https://pawsey.org.au/>)Pawsey Supercomputing Research Centre and (<https://nci.org.au/>)National Computational Infrastructure Australia supercomputer, which have provided modelling to support nano‑level research.
- •
The (<https://pawsey.org.au/>)Pawsey Supercomputing Research Centre, which recently installed a (<https://www.datacenterdynamics.com/en/news/pawsey-supercomputing-centre-installs-room-temperature-diamond-based-quantum-computer/>)room‑temperature (<https://www.datacenterdynamics.com/en/news/pawsey-supercomputing-centre-installs-room-temperature-diamond-based-quantum-computer/>)diamond‑based quantum accelerator that will be used to demonstrate and test hybrid models of quantum and classical computing.

Testing the performance of a nitrogen-vacancy diamond-based vector magnetometer at the University of Melbourne Quantum Magnetometer Test and Measurement Facility. Credit: Chris Lew, University of Melbourne
Case study: Transitioning from the lab to future applications
(<https://www.phasorinnovation.com/>)Phasor Innovation is an Australian quantum business specialising in:
- • radio frequency and microwave engineering
- • electromagnetics
- • system integration
- • quantum technologies.
Phasor Innovation is collaborating with the University of Melbourne and RMIT University on researching and developing the next generation of diamond‑based quantum sensors. There are a range of new and emerging applications for this technology in many areas including the defence, mining, space and medical sectors.
The collaborative university and industry team successfully competed in the inaugural (<https://researchcentre.army.gov.au/library/land-power-forum/army-quantum-technology-challenge-2021>)Army Quantum (<https://researchcentre.army.gov.au/library/land-power-forum/army-quantum-technology-challenge-2021>)Technology Challenge in 2021 and are currently working together on a subsequent project to design, construct, test and evaluate a quantum diamond‑based vector magnetometer that will provide improved surveillance and detection of subterranean targets. The team has also received funding from Defence to further develop the technology for precision magnetic navigation in GNSSdenied environments.

premise_sentence: These regulatory frameworks need to remain fit for purpose and responsive to technological developments.
premise_paragraph:
Quantum technologies could give Australia enormous commercial and sovereign capability advantages. We must protect Australia’s national interests while taking full advantage of the opportunities for Australia’s quantum researchers and industry.
We need to ensure responsible use of quantum technologies. Appropriate controls of these technologies will help protect our research, capabilities and investments.
Australia’s regulatory environment:
- • provides strong protections
- • ensures fair competition
- • supports national interests
- • promotes integrity in the market.
This effective regulatory environment gives certainty to businesses, investors and international partners looking to work with Australia. It also ensures our approach to technologies aligns with our values.
These regulatory frameworks need to remain fit for purpose and responsive to technological developments. This will ensure trust and confidence for investors and support Australia’s national interests.
Developing and using quantum technologies could have significant implications for our national security. These technologies could impact some of our most important national security functions including:
- • defence
- • intelligence
- • encryption
- • sensing and detection
- • computer processing
- • communications.
To increase our resilience, we will need to monitor the development of quantum technologies and their potential impacts on public safety and cyber security. We must address national security challenges and capitalise on opportunities for our exports, capabilities, researchers and companies. Australian academia and industry can contribute to our defence and national security by discovering and building new quantum capabilities. To fully realise those opportunities, we need strong and trusted Australian quantum companies.
Our stakeholders told us that navigating the regulatory environment can be a challenge for advanced and emerging technology industries, including quantum. For example, the necessary focus on mitigating risks and protecting our knowledge and capabilities can inhibit growth and commercial opportunities. Australian technology companies don’t have a large domestic market, which makes access to international markets critical for their survival and growth.

premise_sentence: The quantum industry will also play an important role in improving diversity and inclusion.
premise_paragraph:
Quantum technologies present nearly endless opportunities. But these opportunities must serve the interests of Australian society and contribute to our national wellbeing. The public is increasingly aware of the ethical and social implications of new technologies, and we should not assume they will enthusiastically embrace quantum technologies.
By building principles for responsible development and use, quantum researchers and developers can develop technologies that align to Australian values and expectations and protect human rights. By adopting this approach, Australia can cement itself as a responsible technology developer and attract international companies and investors.
Standards for quantum technologies will give Australians confidence these technologies are being developed and deployed in a way that is trusted, secure and to their benefit. Technology standards and trusted measurement will help grow the sector by creating a competitive supplier market. This will give industry confidence to adopt new technologies and ensure global interoperability.
Standards will provide consistency and opportunities to Australian companies looking to develop or use this technology. With industry leading the way, Australia can have an active role in the global bodies that develop technology standards. This will help ensure that standards for quantum technologies support the growth of a vibrant and competitive marketplace by fostering interoperability, innovation, transparency and security.
The government will continue prioritising Australia’s representation in international quantum standards‑setting bodies and developing principles for responsible and inclusive development and use. This work is consistent with our international obligations and the principles of Australia’s international cyber and critical technology engagement. We will also ensure that developing and adopting these standards doesn’t unnecessarily inhibit research, development and commercialisation of quantum technologies.
To maximise the opportunities of quantum, we must push for greater inclusion and diversity. This includes increasing the participation of women, Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples and other underrepresented groups. The quantum community must engage with rural and regional Australia and connect with families, teachers and children. That way, the whole of Australia can understand and benefit from quantum technologies.
The quantum industry will also play an important role in improving diversity and inclusion. This includes reducing the gender pay gap and supporting women’s participation, particularly in senior positions.

premise_paragraph:
Quantum Communication and Encryption
- • Sparrow Quantum uses single-photon sources to develop quantum hardware for use in scaling advanced applications in quantum information processing, secure communications, quantum computing and the quantum internet.
- • Zybersafe is participating in a field trial with quantum key distribution (QKD), and Cryptomathic and Dencrypt deliver products that support cyber security in larger networks and for mobile devices, respectively.
- • Alea Quantum Technologies has developed a quantum random number generator that can generate random numbers for e.g. encryption.
Quantum Computing and Algorithms
- • Microsoft Development Center is developing quantum materials and topological qubits in their Quantum Materials Lab in Lyngby, Denmark. The laboratory will be at the forefront of developing the world's first scalable quantum computer.
- • Molecular Quantum Solutions is developing quantum chemical calculations using quantum computers in pharma, biotechnology and chemical applications to reduce the need for trivial laboratory experiments.
- • Quantum Machines (QDevil) has developed management and control systems for quantum computers in the form of electronics and low-level software.
- • Kvantify has developed quantum-based software for the pharmaceutical, finance and logistics industries, including a product that can be used in the development of new drugs and solutions that can optimise, e.g., returns from financial portfolios or route plans.
- • QPurpose has developed quantum algorithms and software for use across industries.
- • As part of a research project financed by the Novo Nordisk Foundation, an IBM Quantum Hub is being established at the University of Copenhagen, so that selected researchers, including from businesses, can learn how to program a quantum computer.
Components
- • NKT Photonics is a leading supplier of speciality fiber lasers and Denmark's largest exporter of hardware for the quantum industry.
- • SiPhotonIC manufactures prototypes of Photonic Integrated Circuits, special designed silicon-based microchip that forms a central hardware component in products for quantum optics.
- • With its quantum technology test center, Danish National Metrology Institute (DFM) develops new measurement methods to characterize quantum materials and tests components for use in quantum encryption and quantum sensors.
- • Beamfox Technologies has developed software used by universities and companies for the manufacturing of quantum and nano components.
- • Quantum Foundry Copenhagen is working on developing new quantum materials and components as part of the Novo Nordisk Foundation Quantum Computing Programme (NQCP).

premise_paragraph:
The established vision and defined goal will be fulfilled through the objectives of the main strategic pillars of the National Strategy for Quantum Technologies, which are:
- 1. Research and development
- 2. Education and talent development
- 3. Competitive economy
- 4. International cooperation
- 5. Security and defense
- 6. Quantum technology infrastructure
Responsibility for implementing individual measures lies with a specific manager, who oversees and ensures practical implementation, including cooperation with co-managers. Financial estimates are based on expert assessments and represent the optimal amounts for achieving the objectives. The implementation of individual measures under the strategy is conditional on the allocation of financial resources from the state budget.
Pillar 1: Research and development
For an innovative country such as Czechia, where synergies between cutting-edge research and a strong industrial base can support economic development and global cooperation in the field of emerging technologies, quantum technologies offer significant opportunities for growth and competitiveness.
The national strategy for quantum technologies, its substantive focus, and outputs require cutting-edge basic and applied research and their integration into the European and global quantum ecosystem. It is true and confirmed by practice that strategic technologies are, in principle, linked to high-intensity basic and applied research, quality and organization of research and development, and therefore their support must be directed towards strengthening research excellence, human resources development and, subsequently, effective technology and knowledge transfer. Czechia has a strong background in basic research, which has promising potential for applied research in the areas of quantum computing, quantum computer technologies and simulators on various physical platforms, quantum communication, quantum sensors, quantum metrology, and new quantum materials. Research centers are concentrated in various types of research organizations and cooperate across the country. In order to develop current competencies, accelerate and develop quantum technology research, and achieve the required competitiveness on the global stage, intensive and sustainable support for basic and applied research is necessary, similar to that in economically comparable countries (Denmark, the Netherlands, Finland, etc.). Support for links to European and international research, support for the transfer of acquired knowledge into practice, and support for the creation of start-ups, spin-offs, and new companies engaged in research and applications of quantum technologies. Given the declared military and security nature of quantum technologies, cooperation within the EU (EDA, EDF) and NATO (STO, DIANA, NIF) must also be taken into account, including the secondment of experts and representatives to these organizations.
In addition, it is equally necessary to set significantly higher standards for evaluating one's own work, performance, and results by comparing and adopting international benchmarks at the level of global best practice, both in one's own scientific and research work and in its organization and the transfer of results into practice.
Support for participation in international research programs, consortia, and infrastructures is essential for the Czech Republic's international competitiveness and national security, not only in civil areas but also in defense and security, where national co-financing is often required. Given the dual nature of quantum technologies, it is assumed that the results of publicly funded research and development may also be used in defense or security research in accordance with the Czech Republic's legal system and its international obligations. Funding for quantum technologies should be stable and strategically aligned with priority areas of focus (quantum science, quantum computing and simulation, quantum communication, quantum sensing, quantum metrology, and quantum materials). It is necessary to ensure that new activities contribute to the further development of existing cutting-edge research centers in the Czech Republic and, where necessary, to the creation of new ones.
STRATEGIC OBJECTIVE
Czechia has top-class research facilities in a wide range of quantum sciences, quantum computing, quantum communication, quantum sensor technology, quantum metrology, and quantum materials, which are involved in the European and global quantum ecosystem.
MEASURES TO ACHIEVE THE OBJECTIVE
P1.1
A targeted support program focused on supporting excellent scientific teams in the field of quantum technologies.
Description
Prepare and implement a targeted support program for research, development, and innovation with the aim of supporting excellent scientific teams with an emphasis on strengthening interdisciplinarity and national cooperation in research and development and creating conditions for the development of human resources in research and development concentrated around important domestic and foreign personalities. By 2025, a public competition will be prepared, and funding will commence in 2026.
Responsibility

Cooperation

Pillar 2: Education and training of talent
The education and training of talented individuals is a key and essential prerequisite for the development of the quantum ecosystem in Czechia. The success of in the field of quantum technologies is critically dependent on Czechia's ability to educate, attract, and retain a skilled workforce. It is essential to support not only experts in currently known applications (e.g., quantum computers, communications, sensors), but also to create flexible and adaptable education and training capacities for the future use of quantum technologies in areas that we cannot yet fully imagine (e.g., new types of materials, bio-quantum applications, advanced medical technologies, or quantum aspects of artificial intelligence). In the long term, it is crucial that the Czech Republic has not only top scientists and engineers, but also a sufficient number of technicians, operators, and managers capable of understanding the principles of quantum technologies and integrating them into everyday practice. The main effort should be focused on maximizing support for linking education in the field of quantum technologies with research, the corporate sector, and the popularization of quantum technologies throughout society, including in the area of defense and security. The educational ecosystem must be based on the education of highly qualified specialists in master's and doctoral study programs. This education is closely linked to cutting-edge research in quantum sciences and technologies and related fields of physics and technical sciences. Specialists in the field of quantum technologies, including foreign teachers, need to be involved. Educational programs should cover not only the highly abstract areas of theoretical quantum computing and quantum informatics, but also study programs devoted to the experimental aspects of quantum materials and the experimental fundamentals of quantum physics and other related areas. It is necessary to offer clearly conceived courses on the basics of quantum technologies as part of the general foundation of bachelor's study programs, not only at technical or natural science faculties, but also at humanities faculties. Lifelong learning courses, especially micro-certificate programs, will play an important role. Their systematic use is expected by companies involved in quantum technologies or workplaces specializing in measurement technology or the development of experimental solutions. A special target group will be the state administration, or state employees. Universities and their institutes, institutes of the Czech Academy of Sciences, the Confederation of Industry of the Czech Republic, the Chamber of Commerce, companies, start-ups, and spin-offs will be involved in the implementation of educational activities.
It is essential to pay attention to the education of civil servants who will be involved in the implementation of this strategy. Furthermore, current and future teachers need to be educated in the basics, key principles, and applications of quantum technologies. It is necessary to develop a range of teaching aids for future teachers as well as pupils and students. Take advantage of existing free software and platforms that enable quantum computing simulation. Schools will need to be equipped with experimental teaching kits that will allow pupils and students to study the phenomena of quantum physics and mechanics directly. It is also necessary to motivate children, pupils, and secondary school students to take an interest in quantum technologies. Activities that are suitable for this purpose include those carried out in cooperation with universities, secondary schools, and elementary schools (popularization lectures, laboratory visits, excursions, various competitions, etc.). Furthermore, the existing network of science centers should be used for popularization programs and attractive exhibits. A major advantage of the Czech Republic is its long-standing and sophisticated system of professional competitions. The topic of quantum technologies should be gradually incorporated into these competitions. It is also necessary to use the media and social networks to promote quantum technologies in a positive light.
STRATEGIC OBJECTIVE
Czechia is capable of providing education and training for experts and users of quantum technologies. By the end of 2031, the Czech Republic will be able to produce 150 graduates of university study programs in the field of quantum technologies annually.
MEASURES TO ACHIEVE THE OBJECTIVE
P2.1
Program to support the creation and development of bachelor's, master's, and doctoral study programs in the field of quantum technologies.
Description
Prepare and subsequently implement a program to support the creation and development of bachelor's, master's, and doctoral study programs (including teacher training) in the field of quantum technologies. The aim of this measure is to increase education capacity in quantum technologies. The program should systematically build a base of qualified human resources capable of working with quantum technologies at all levels, from research and development through practical engineering applications to implementation in industry and government. and safety.
Responsibility
Ministry of Education

This includes the preparation of further training for teaching staff, as well as projects
informal education and knowledge support for talented individuals, interest groups, competitions, excursions, and other leisure activities for primary and secondary school students. A special group will be formed by state administration employees.

Pillar 3: Competitive economy
The economic power of the quantum ecosystem is a significant boost to overall economic performance. Value creation is driven by a combination of innovative research, quantum technology development, and the commercialization of these technologies into market-attractive products and services. The value created in the field of quantum technologies is not only derived from direct economic returns, but also from the strategic advantages that these technologies bring, ranging from breakthrough computing power and secure communications to high-precision measurements and robust integration into global supply chains. Strengthening the economy through quantum technologies also increases the resilience of the state, its security, and its ability to respond to new threats and opportunities. The strength of the ecosystem is further enhanced by robust cooperation between the public and private sectors, creating an environment in which quantum start-ups and spin-offs can thrive alongside established technology giants. However, maintaining and growing this economic strength requires ongoing stable investment and systematic support for education and talent development. Czechia should strive to be among the countries capable not only of using quantum technologies, but also of independently developing and creating their essential parts. This requires synergistic cooperation between higher education and research institutions, the corporate sector, and government. A necessary prerequisite is support for the entire chain, from basic and applied research to technology transfer and the creation of start-ups and spin-offs, to support for small and medium-sized enterprises and incentives for renowned companies. The key will be to focus on supporting the systematic involvement of companies and professional organizations in the process of finding applications for quantum technologies. Early awareness-raising will play a key role here, with the aim of a gradual and persistently organized search for practical applications from both a user perspective and, in particular, from a manufacturing or value-added perspective. To this end, entities will be systematically approached, followed by the creation of a communication network. The connection to the corporate sector will also be emphasized in the measures of Pillar 1. Cooperation between the academic, corporate, and public sectors will not only move quantum technologies towards real-world applications and adoption, but will also ensure that Czechia maintains its defense capabilities and security and remains competitive in terms of knowledge and economy. Technologies developed in research institutions in particular have the potential to contribute to the development of quantum computers and their components. This opens up an opportunity to master quantum computing technology on selected physical platforms at the national level and to be among the countries capable of producing quantum computers or their key components. The main goal is to ensure that advances in quantum technologies are effectively translated into tangible, market-ready solutions, thereby strengthening Czechia's position in the global technology environment. It is also necessary to ensure that all state institutions, public administration, and the wider public are informed about the opportunities and risks associated with quantum technologies and are prepared and able to use, implement, and support quantum technologies.
STRATEGIC OBJECTIVE
Thanks to its system of support for the entire chain, from basic and applied research to technology transfer and the creation of start-ups and spin-offs, as well as support for small and medium-sized enterprises and incentives for large companies, Czechia is an attractive place for doing business in the field of quantum technologies.
MEASURES TO ACHIEVE THE OBJECTIVE

Pillar 4 International Cooperation
International cooperation plays a key role in the success of the overall development of quantum technologies. The challenges posed by the development of quantum technologies cannot be addressed without broad and long-term international cooperation. The main objective is cooperation within the EU, NATO, the European area, and with countries around the world that share the same democratic values. One of the main benefits of international cooperation is the sharing of expertise and experience, which is essential for maintaining competitiveness in the rapidly evolving field of quantum technologies. Access to the latest knowledge and research methods, often available through leading research institutions and universities, is therefore essential. This also applies to access to the most advanced quantum computers for research, development, and education purposes. Cooperation with leading research institutions and universities, not only in the European Research Area but also in other advanced democratic countries, is key. Support for participation in international research collaborations, foreign investment, and funding is an important element in the development of quantum technologies. Such cooperation and investment not only increase the capacity to carry out large-scale research projects, but also enable the development of advanced research and testing infrastructures necessary for experimental work and innovation. This includes investment in laboratories, testing facilities, and computing resources that are key to exploring and exploiting quantum phenomena and technologies. These partnerships enable Czech research teams and companies to join broader partner networks, which are essential for sharing best practices and experiences. Through these networks, it is possible to collaborate on joint projects, share unique research resources and approaches, and thus create synergies that exceed the capabilities of individual countries. Special attention will also be paid to European countries where the Ministry of Education, Youth and Sports and the Czech Science Foundation do not currently have agreements on scientific projects beyond mobility (France, Spain, Finland, Denmark, the Netherlands). Cooperation with global partners can help Czech start-ups, spin-offs, and companies bridge the gap between research and the market, provide access to new markets and customers, and create new channels for monetizing technologies, products, and services.
International cooperation in the field of quantum technologies also promotes the exchange of talent and expertise. Establishing joint business partnerships, research programs, and projects, and sharing expertise can significantly contribute to the development of domestic capacities for the development of the quantum ecosystem.
STRATEGIC OBJECTIVE
Strengthen international cooperation Czechia in the field of quantum technologies with an emphasis on partnerships in the European Research Area and with democratic countries around the world, so that Czech research teams, companies, and institutions gain access to the latest knowledge, infrastructure, investments, and markets, enabling them to effectively develop the domestic quantum ecosystem and increase their global competitiveness. Czechia is a sought-after and a reliable partner for research and industrial cooperation within the European and global quantum ecosystem.
MEASURES TO ACHIEVE THE OBJECTIVE
P4.1

Pillar 5: Security and defense
NATO emphasizes the disruptive potential of quantum technologies in the fields of cryptography, quantum computing, and quantum sensing, and calls on its members to accelerate the development of quantum technologies for military applications. Many current cryptographic methods based on complex mathematical problems may be broken by quantum computers in the future. It is therefore crucial to focus on the introduction of new quantum-resistant cryptographic algorithms (PQC) that are practically applicable and internationally supported. PQC is currently the main tool for defending against quantum attacks in the coming years. At the same time, it is necessary to address the use of quantum key distribution (QKD) to enhance communication security and build quantum networks. Quantum technologies offer revolutionary approaches to navigation and detection, especially in environments where traditional GPS signals may be unavailable or disrupted. Quantum sensors can provide highly accurate and robust methods for measuring a range of physical quantities, and quantum metrology offers significant improvements in navigation and data operations, which is essential for modern military operations. Quantum simulations can enable more accurate modeling of complex systems and scenarios, which is key to effective logistics and management in both civilian and military contexts. Quantum technologies are dual-use technologies. They offer both civilian and military applications, which significantly increases the demands on their protection. It is essential that Czechia implement thorough measures to protect these technologies, including protecting their research and development from threats such as espionage and cyber attacks. This includes building defensive experimental and testing infrastructures that will enable the verification of quantum technologies in realistic conditions, for example for military navigation without GPS, detection of stealth objects, or advanced simulations. Cooperation with security and intelligence agencies, which can identify potential threats and provide timely warnings of specific risks, is key, as is coordination of activities with alliance partners (NATO, EU) to ensure the interoperability of quantum systems and the sharing of sensitive knowledge in accordance with international commitments. The emphasis on security is essential not only for the protection of the state's strategic interests, but also for ensuring competitive advantage, critical infrastructure resilience, and the Czech Republic's sovereignty in the field of quantum technologies on the international stage. It is therefore crucial to ensure conditions for effective research and application cooperation between all stakeholders, build military testing infrastructure, develop interoperability with international partners, and strengthen the protection of critical systems in the Czech Republic with regard to the quantum threat. The development and integration of these technologies should be managed and coordinated at all levels of government, with the Ministry of Defense playing a key role in cooperation with the Ministry of the Interior, the National Cyber and Information Security Agency, and the intelligence services.
STRATEGIC OBJECTIVE
Use quantum technologies and PQC to ensure the resilience, security, and defense capabilities of the state through the development of quantum-resistant and secure communications, advanced navigation, detection, and simulations by ensuring coordinated cooperation in research, development, innovation, and education between government, research institutions, industry, and international partners.

Pillar 6: Quantum technology infrastructure
For Czechia, building a cutting-edge infrastructure for quantum technologies is essential not only in terms of scientific progress, but also for long-term competitiveness in the European and global context. This infrastructure is the basis for testing and accelerating the use of quantum technologies in industry and other sectors.
Thanks to its involvement in European initiatives such as EuroHPC, Quantum Flagship, and Chips JU-Quantum Chips Pilot lines, Czechia has a unique opportunity to become an important player in the field of quantum technologies. The national infrastructure, based on the principles of open access, also supports the involvement of the corporate sector, interdisciplinary cooperation, education, and the development of qualified experts, and strengthens Czechia's involvement in international research projects, thereby contributing to the sustainable development of the knowledge economy and the country's technological sovereignty.
Access to advanced quantum infrastructure will not only enable cutting-edge research, but above all faster adoption and application of these technologies in industry, the creation of new applications, products, and services with high added value. For the public sector, the involvement of the State part of the eGovernment cloud will also need to be ensured in building quantum technology infrastructure capacities.
Within the framework of existing and newly developed instruments, it is necessary to support the acquisition of investments and operating funds for equipment and infrastructure for quantum technologies and supporting technologies, particularly in the area of scalability of demanding quantum computers, quantum simulators, and also in the areas of quantum communication towards multi-user networks, in the area of massive development of quantum sensors, metrology, and new quantum materials, thereby significantly strengthening targeted measures for the creation, testing, and implementation of these technologies in practice.
STRATEGIC OBJECTIVE
Czechia has a cutting-edge infrastructure for quantum technologies, particularly in the areas of quantum computers, quantum communication infrastructure, quantum sensors, metrology, and materials. This infrastructure is widely available and serves the needs of research and development, education, industry, and the public sector in the Czech Republic, as well as supporting international cooperation.

5.1. Risk
This chapter deals with the identification of risks that threaten the achievement of the objectives of the National Strategy for Quantum Technologies and the possibilities for mitigating them.
Key factors include securing sufficient financial resources for the entire duration of the strategy's implementation, which will enable the implementation of the strategy's steps and the training and recruitment of a sufficient number of experts who will be able to develop the field in research, industry, and public administration. The financial profile is based on the timing of the necessary activities and measures. In doing so, it is necessary to pay close attention to maintaining competitive conditions for retaining and recruiting experts. The entire process of implementing the National Strategy for Quantum Technologies depends heavily on training and recruiting a sufficient number of experts in the field of quantum technologies. Support and funding for the development and operation of research infrastructure in the field is also essential. Limiting the strategy in these areas would cause future damage to the national economy as well as to security and defense capabilities. Table 3 represents a simplified matrix of global risks. premise_paragraph:
Quantum technology is currently being introduced in several of the EU's space programs and the European Space Agency's (ESA's) space programs with a focus on i.a. providing better knowledge about climate change, access to secure communication and improved monitoring of traffic in space. Quantum technology also has potential to support the green transition – In both the energy and transport sectors - through better and faster processing of data from satellites.
The EU has launched an initiative with the purpose of investigating the possibilities of equipping future Earth observation satellites with quantum technology. In an initiative between the EU and ESA, we see e.g. new types of sensors based on quantum technology as a promising method for optimizing gravity measurements, which is also used for direct measurement of global sea level rise, melting of the earth's ice caps and the cycle of droughts and floods.
In the long term, space-based quantum technology is expected to support the European space programs and contribute to improved models for climate development and its resulting effects.

Quantum technology can contribute to increasing the safety and resilience of satellite-based services and, especially, in warning about the risk of collisions between satellites. With the prospect of a rapidly growing number of satellites in space, the need for automation is urgent.
The government therefore wants to prioritize the quantum field, also in connection with Danish participation in program areas in, for example, ESA's earth observation and communications programs. At the same time, the government wants to support Danish participation in the EU's programs in the space sector, e.g. EuroQCI.
KEY FOCUS AREA 3: Improved Access to Digital Research Infrastructure
Access to research infrastructure (e.g. equipment, laboratories and computing power) is essential to the potential for research to expand boundaries and possibilities for creating ground-breaking results. The infrastructure also plays an important role for education, recruitment and retention of talent at Danish research institutions.
Also research infrastructure for e.g. the production and testing of quantum technology, as well as measurement and calibration techniques, is crucial for the development of international standards for new quantum products. Access to facilities at the universities and at the Research and Technology Organisations (GTS-institutes) is also of great importance to companies that may need to test technology before it can be marketed.
Denmark has a good starting point in the current research and test infrastructure, i.a. through earmarked funding from the national pool for research infrastructure. In addition, the Danish universities and GTS-institutes, etc. have made several investments in research infrastructure with the support from both public and private funding. This also applies to Danish participation in a number of international infrastructure initiatives, e.g. the Danish co-hosting of the material research facility European Spallation Source (ESS). Furthermore, Denmark has established a national cooperation on digital research infrastructure by setting up Danish e-Infrastructure Cooperation (DeiC), which is a cooperation of Denmark's eight universities and the Danish Agency for Higher Education and Science. The government wants the strategic quantum program, cf. initiative 1, to support test and demonstration facilities in Denmark.
The need for up-to-date and strong data processing systems is increasing, and the digital research infrastructure has become significantly more complex and resource-intensive. On top of that greater demands being placed on the physical and digital security and risk management surrounding the infrastructure. This increases the costs of establishing adequate access for researchers and students.

Responsibility for implementing individual measures lies with a specific manager, who oversees and ensures practical implementation, including cooperation with co-managers. Financial estimates are based on expert assessments and represent the optimal amounts for achieving the objectives. The implementation of individual measures under the strategy is conditional on the allocation of financial resources from the state budget.
Pillar 1: Research and development
For an innovative country such as Czechia, where synergies between cutting-edge research and a strong industrial base can support economic development and global cooperation in the field of emerging technologies, quantum technologies offer significant opportunities for growth and competitiveness.
The national strategy for quantum technologies, its substantive focus, and outputs require cutting-edge basic and applied research and their integration into the European and global quantum ecosystem. It is true and confirmed by practice that strategic technologies are, in principle, linked to high-intensity basic and applied research, quality and organization of research and development, and therefore their support must be directed towards strengthening research excellence, human resources development and, subsequently, effective technology and knowledge transfer. Czechia has a strong background in basic research, which has promising potential for applied research in the areas of quantum computing, quantum computer technologies and simulators on various physical platforms, quantum communication, quantum sensors, quantum metrology, and new quantum materials. Research centers are concentrated in various types of research organizations and cooperate across the country. In order to develop current competencies, accelerate and develop quantum technology research, and achieve the required competitiveness on the global stage, intensive and sustainable support for basic and applied research is necessary, similar to that in economically comparable countries (Denmark, the Netherlands, Finland, etc.). Support for links to European and international research, support for the transfer of acquired knowledge into practice, and support for the creation of start-ups, spin-offs, and new companies engaged in research and applications of quantum technologies. Given the declared military and security nature of quantum technologies, cooperation within the EU (EDA, EDF) and NATO (STO, DIANA, NIF) must also be taken into account, including the secondment of experts and representatives to these organizations.
In addition, it is equally necessary to set significantly higher standards for evaluating one's own work, performance, and results by comparing and adopting international benchmarks at the level of global best practice, both in one's own scientific and research work and in its organization and the transfer of results into practice.
Support for participation in international research programs, consortia, and infrastructures is essential for the Czech Republic's international competitiveness and national security, not only in civil areas but also in defense and security, where national co-financing is often required. Given the dual nature of quantum technologies, it is assumed that the results of publicly funded research and development may also be used in defense or security research in accordance with the Czech Republic's legal system and its international obligations. Funding for quantum technologies should be stable and strategically aligned with priority areas of focus (quantum science, quantum computing and simulation, quantum communication, quantum sensing, quantum metrology, and quantum materials). It is necessary to ensure that new activities contribute to the further development of existing cutting-edge research centers in the Czech Republic and, where necessary, to the creation of new ones.
STRATEGIC OBJECTIVE
Czechia has top-class research facilities in a wide range of quantum sciences, quantum computing, quantum communication, quantum sensor technology, quantum metrology, and quantum materials, which are involved in the European and global quantum ecosystem.

Pillar 2: Education and training of talent
The education and training of talented individuals is a key and essential prerequisite for the development of the quantum ecosystem in Czechia. The success of in the field of quantum technologies is critically dependent on Czechia's ability to educate, attract, and retain a skilled workforce. It is essential to support not only experts in currently known applications (e.g., quantum computers, communications, sensors), but also to create flexible and adaptable education and training capacities for the future use of quantum technologies in areas that we cannot yet fully imagine (e.g., new types of materials, bio-quantum applications, advanced medical technologies, or quantum aspects of artificial intelligence). In the long term, it is crucial that the Czech Republic has not only top scientists and engineers, but also a sufficient number of technicians, operators, and managers capable of understanding the principles of quantum technologies and integrating them into everyday practice. The main effort should be focused on maximizing support for linking education in the field of quantum technologies with research, the corporate sector, and the popularization of quantum technologies throughout society, including in the area of defense and security. The educational ecosystem must be based on the education of highly qualified specialists in master's and doctoral study programs. This education is closely linked to cutting-edge research in quantum sciences and technologies and related fields of physics and technical sciences. Specialists in the field of quantum technologies, including foreign teachers, need to be involved. Educational programs should cover not only the highly abstract areas of theoretical quantum computing and quantum informatics, but also study programs devoted to the experimental aspects of quantum materials and the experimental fundamentals of quantum physics and other related areas. It is necessary to offer clearly conceived courses on the basics of quantum technologies as part of the general foundation of bachelor's study programs, not only at technical or natural science faculties, but also at humanities faculties. Lifelong learning courses, especially micro-certificate programs, will play an important role. Their systematic use is expected by companies involved in quantum technologies or workplaces specializing in measurement technology or the development of experimental solutions. A special target group will be the state administration, or state employees. Universities and their institutes, institutes of the Czech Academy of Sciences, the Confederation of Industry of the Czech Republic, the Chamber of Commerce, companies, start-ups, and spin-offs will be involved in the implementation of educational activities.
It is essential to pay attention to the education of civil servants who will be involved in the implementation of this strategy. Furthermore, current and future teachers need to be educated in the basics, key principles, and applications of quantum technologies. It is necessary to develop a range of teaching aids for future teachers as well as pupils and students. Take advantage of existing free software and platforms that enable quantum computing simulation. Schools will need to be equipped with experimental teaching kits that will allow pupils and students to study the phenomena of quantum physics and mechanics directly. It is also necessary to motivate children, pupils, and secondary school students to take an interest in quantum technologies. Activities that are suitable for this purpose include those carried out in cooperation with universities, secondary schools, and elementary schools (popularization lectures, laboratory visits, excursions, various competitions, etc.). Furthermore, the existing network of science centers should be used for popularization programs and attractive exhibits. A major advantage of the Czech Republic is its long-standing and sophisticated system of professional competitions. The topic of quantum technologies should be gradually incorporated into these competitions. It is also necessary to use the media and social networks to promote quantum technologies in a positive light.
STRATEGIC OBJECTIVE
Czechia is capable of providing education and training for experts and users of quantum technologies. By the end of 2031, the Czech Republic will be able to produce 150 graduates of university study programs in the field of quantum technologies annually.

Pillar 3: Competitive economy
The economic power of the quantum ecosystem is a significant boost to overall economic performance. Value creation is driven by a combination of innovative research, quantum technology development, and the commercialization of these technologies into market-attractive products and services. The value created in the field of quantum technologies is not only derived from direct economic returns, but also from the strategic advantages that these technologies bring, ranging from breakthrough computing power and secure communications to high-precision measurements and robust integration into global supply chains. Strengthening the economy through quantum technologies also increases the resilience of the state, its security, and its ability to respond to new threats and opportunities. The strength of the ecosystem is further enhanced by robust cooperation between the public and private sectors, creating an environment in which quantum start-ups and spin-offs can thrive alongside established technology giants. However, maintaining and growing this economic strength requires ongoing stable investment and systematic support for education and talent development. Czechia should strive to be among the countries capable not only of using quantum technologies, but also of independently developing and creating their essential parts. This requires synergistic cooperation between higher education and research institutions, the corporate sector, and government. A necessary prerequisite is support for the entire chain, from basic and applied research to technology transfer and the creation of start-ups and spin-offs, to support for small and medium-sized enterprises and incentives for renowned companies. The key will be to focus on supporting the systematic involvement of companies and professional organizations in the process of finding applications for quantum technologies. Early awareness-raising will play a key role here, with the aim of a gradual and persistently organized search for practical applications from both a user perspective and, in particular, from a manufacturing or value-added perspective. To this end, entities will be systematically approached, followed by the creation of a communication network. The connection to the corporate sector will also be emphasized in the measures of Pillar 1. Cooperation between the academic, corporate, and public sectors will not only move quantum technologies towards real-world applications and adoption, but will also ensure that Czechia maintains its defense capabilities and security and remains competitive in terms of knowledge and economy. Technologies developed in research institutions in particular have the potential to contribute to the development of quantum computers and their components. This opens up an opportunity to master quantum computing technology on selected physical platforms at the national level and to be among the countries capable of producing quantum computers or their key components. The main goal is to ensure that advances in quantum technologies are effectively translated into tangible, market-ready solutions, thereby strengthening Czechia's position in the global technology environment. It is also necessary to ensure that all state institutions, public administration, and the wider public are informed about the opportunities and risks associated with quantum technologies and are prepared and able to use, implement, and support quantum technologies.
STRATEGIC OBJECTIVE
Thanks to its system of support for the entire chain, from basic and applied research to technology transfer and the creation of start-ups and spin-offs, as well as support for small and medium-sized enterprises and incentives for large companies, Czechia is an attractive place for doing business in the field of quantum technologies.

Pillar 4 International Cooperation
International cooperation plays a key role in the success of the overall development of quantum technologies. The challenges posed by the development of quantum technologies cannot be addressed without broad and long-term international cooperation. The main objective is cooperation within the EU, NATO, the European area, and with countries around the world that share the same democratic values. One of the main benefits of international cooperation is the sharing of expertise and experience, which is essential for maintaining competitiveness in the rapidly evolving field of quantum technologies. Access to the latest knowledge and research methods, often available through leading research institutions and universities, is therefore essential. This also applies to access to the most advanced quantum computers for research, development, and education purposes. Cooperation with leading research institutions and universities, not only in the European Research Area but also in other advanced democratic countries, is key. Support for participation in international research collaborations, foreign investment, and funding is an important element in the development of quantum technologies. Such cooperation and investment not only increase the capacity to carry out large-scale research projects, but also enable the development of advanced research and testing infrastructures necessary for experimental work and innovation. This includes investment in laboratories, testing facilities, and computing resources that are key to exploring and exploiting quantum phenomena and technologies. These partnerships enable Czech research teams and companies to join broader partner networks, which are essential for sharing best practices and experiences. Through these networks, it is possible to collaborate on joint projects, share unique research resources and approaches, and thus create synergies that exceed the capabilities of individual countries. Special attention will also be paid to European countries where the Ministry of Education, Youth and Sports and the Czech Science Foundation do not currently have agreements on scientific projects beyond mobility (France, Spain, Finland, Denmark, the Netherlands). Cooperation with global partners can help Czech start-ups, spin-offs, and companies bridge the gap between research and the market, provide access to new markets and customers, and create new channels for monetizing technologies, products, and services.
International cooperation in the field of quantum technologies also promotes the exchange of talent and expertise. Establishing joint business partnerships, research programs, and projects, and sharing expertise can significantly contribute to the development of domestic capacities for the development of the quantum ecosystem.
STRATEGIC OBJECTIVE
Strengthen international cooperation Czechia in the field of quantum technologies with an emphasis on partnerships in the European Research Area and with democratic countries around the world, so that Czech research teams, companies, and institutions gain access to the latest knowledge, infrastructure, investments, and markets, enabling them to effectively develop the domestic quantum ecosystem and increase their global competitiveness. Czechia is a sought-after and a reliable partner for research and industrial cooperation within the European and global quantum ecosystem.
